bio_short_en: Abu Huraira (RA) was one of the most prolific narrators of hadith in Islamic history, transmitting over 5,000 narrations. A companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), he embraced Islam and devoted himself entirely to the Prophet's company, memorizing his sayings and deeds with extraordinary dedication. He accompanied the Prophet during the later years of his life and prioritized learning over worldly pursuits. His vast memory and tireless narration made him an indispensable source for the Sunnah, and scholars of hadith across generations relied heavily on his transmissions.

Aisha (RA), daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), was one of the most knowledgeable companions and a major source of hadith. She narrated thousands of traditions, particularly on matters of the Prophet's private life, worship, and character. Scholars and companions regularly consulted her on religious rulings, and she was known for her sharp intellect and willingness to correct misunderstandings. Her narrations are foundational to Islamic jurisprudence, especially in areas of family law, purification, and personal conduct.

Anas ibn Malik (RA) served the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) for ten years from the age of ten until the Prophet's death. A companion from Medina, he was one of the youngest major hadith narrators and lived to an advanced age, allowing him to transmit thousands of narrations to later generations. He is among the seven companions known as the Mukthirun — those who narrated the most hadith. His close personal service to the Prophet gave him unique insight into the Prophet's daily habits, worship, and manners.

Abdullah ibn Umar (RA), son of the second caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab, was among the most prolific and scrupulous hadith narrators among the companions. He embraced Islam as a child and participated in major military campaigns. Known for his extreme caution in narrating hadith and his strict adherence to the Prophet's Sunnah in every detail of life, he was widely respected for his piety and precision. His narrations number in the thousands and are found throughout the major hadith collections.

Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, was born three years before the Hijra and died in 68 AH. Nicknamed Hibr al-Ummah (the Scholar of the Ummah) and Tarjuman al-Quran (the Interpreter of the Quran), he narrated over 1,600 hadiths. The Prophet ﷺ made special supplication for him: 'O Allah, teach him wisdom and the interpretation of the Quran.' He became the foremost authority on Quranic exegesis among the Companions, and thousands of students traveled to learn from him. He is considered the founder of the tafsir tradition in Islam.

Jabir ibn Abdullah (RA) was an Ansari companion from Medina who narrated over 1,500 hadiths covering worship, transactions, the Prophet's biography, and many other topics. He participated in numerous military expeditions alongside the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and was known for his close companionship and reliability as a narrator. He lived into the era of the Tabi'in and continued teaching and transmitting prophetic knowledge until late in his life, making him one of the most cited companion narrators in the major hadith collections.

Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) was among the earliest Muslims and one of the most learned companions in Quranic recitation and Islamic jurisprudence. He was so close to the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) that he served as his personal attendant in many matters. The Prophet described him as one whose guidance should be followed. He was a primary teacher in Kufa, and his students became leading scholars of the next generation. His narrations are especially valued for their precision and deep legal insight.

Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who was uniquely permitted by the Prophet to write down hadiths, making him one of the earliest systematic recorders of prophetic traditions. He was known for his intense devotion to worship and learning, and he narrated hundreds of hadiths on a wide range of religious topics. He also had knowledge of earlier scriptures, which gave him a broad perspective on religious matters. His narrations are found in all major hadith collections.

Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and the fourth Caliph of Islam. He was among the first to embrace Islam and was raised in the Prophet's household, giving him unparalleled access to prophetic knowledge and daily practice. Renowned for his scholarship, piety, and bravery, he narrated numerous hadiths and is considered one of the most authoritative sources on prophetic traditions, particularly in matters of jurisprudence, prayer, and governance.

Abu Musa al-Ashari (RA) was a companion from Yemen who emigrated to Medina and became a devoted follower of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He served as a governor in both Basra and Kufa and was known for his exceptionally beautiful recitation of the Quran, a quality the Prophet himself praised and admired. A prolific narrator, he transmitted hundreds of hadiths and was greatly respected by the companions and Tabi'in for his religious knowledge, upright character, and dedicated service to Islam.

Abu Said al-Khudri (RA), whose full name was Sa'd ibn Malik ibn Sinan al-Ansari, was born in Madinah and lived until 74 AH. He participated in twelve military expeditions with the Prophet ﷺ and became one of the most prolific transmitters of hadith, narrating over 1,170 traditions. He was particularly known for his detailed transmission of the Prophet's guidance on acts of worship, spiritual purification, and social conduct. He remained in Madinah throughout his life, teaching hadith and serving as a reference for the Companions and Successors on religious matters.

Nafi was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and freed slave of Abdullah ibn Umar (RA). He is one of the most important links in the chain of hadith transmission, particularly in the famous isnad known as the 'Golden Chain' — Malik from Nafi from Ibn Umar. He served Ibn Umar closely for many years and transmitted a vast body of prophetic narrations on his authority. Imam Malik considered him among the most trustworthy of narrators, and his reports are found throughout the major hadith collections and are held in the highest regard.

Sahl ibn Sa'd al-Sa'idi (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who lived to an exceptional age, reportedly becoming one of the last surviving companions in Medina. His long life allowed him to narrate prophetic traditions to generations of Tabi'in who had not witnessed the Prophet directly, making him a vital link in the chain of transmission. He transmitted narrations covering various aspects of the Prophet's life, worship, and character, and is regarded as a trustworthy and valued narrator.

Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was a Companion from the Ansar of Madinah who participated in eighteen military expeditions alongside the Prophet ﷺ. Though he was initially too young to participate in the Battle of Badr — the Prophet ﷺ sent him back due to his young age — he later took part in all subsequent major campaigns. He narrated approximately 305 hadiths covering various aspects of worship, conduct, and the Prophet's character. He settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death and was highly regarded as a transmitter in Iraq.

AbuSa'id al-Khudri (RA) was an Ansari companion and prolific narrator of hadith who transmitted hundreds of prophetic traditions across a wide range of religious topics. He was devoted to preserving and spreading the Sunnah after the Prophet Muhammad's (peace be upon him) death and is particularly noted for narrations related to worship, eschatology, and the signs of the Last Day. His dedication to accuracy and his close companionship with the Prophet made him a trusted authority among subsequent scholars of hadith.

Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), cousin of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), was the foremost Quranic exegete of his generation and is known by the title 'Scholar of the Ummah.' The Prophet prayed specifically for him to be granted wisdom and deep understanding of the religion. Ibn Abbas narrated hundreds of hadiths and his explanations of the Quran are foundational to classical tafsir literature. He gathered knowledge from senior companions after the Prophet's death, becoming an encyclopedic authority recognized across all schools of Islamic scholarship.

Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina. He narrated a large number of hadiths and was known for his commitment to transmitting the Prophet's words accurately and completely. His narrations span a wide range of religious topics including prayer, purification, charity, and the afterlife, and are considered authoritative in the hadith sciences. He continued teaching and narrating after the Prophet's death, serving as an important link to the prophetic tradition for subsequent generations.

Urwa ibn al-Zubayr was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and the son of al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, a companion of the Prophet (peace be upon him). As the nephew of Aisha (RA), he was one of the most important transmitters of her extensive narrations and a primary source for knowledge of the Prophet's household. Urwa was among the Seven Jurists of Medina, a recognized group of Tabi'in scholars whose scholarly opinions shaped early Islamic jurisprudence. His narrations appear frequently in all major hadith collections and he is regarded as highly reliable.

Aishah (RA), the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, was one of the greatest scholarly authorities in the early Muslim community. She narrated thousands of hadiths, particularly on matters of the Prophet's personal conduct, worship, and household life. Companions and Tabi'in regularly sought her rulings on religious questions, and she was known for her willingness to correct errors in narration. Her deep understanding of Islamic law and her proximity to the Prophet made her an unparalleled source of authentic prophetic tradition.

Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) was one of the earliest converts to Islam, embracing the faith while still a young man in Makkah. He was so close to the Prophet ﷺ that he was given permission to enter the Prophet's home without seeking permission and described as one who resembled the Prophet ﷺ most closely in his walk and conduct. He narrated 848 hadiths and was appointed by Umar (RA) as a teacher and judge in Kufa. He is particularly renowned for his Quranic recitation and is considered one of the four great authorities on the Quran among the Companions.

Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA) was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the most learned scholars among the companions, known by the title 'Scholar of the Ummah.' He devoted his life to Quranic scholarship and hadith narration. His narrations and tafsir explanations are central references in Islamic scholarship across all traditions and legal schools. He gathered knowledge diligently from senior companions after the Prophet's death and became an encyclopedic authority whose contributions remain indispensable to Islamic learning.

Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA) was one of the earliest Muslims and among the most ascetic and outspoken of the Prophet Muhammad's (peace be upon him) companions. He was known for his strong stance against worldly attachment, accumulation of wealth, and injustice. He narrated numerous hadiths and is particularly remembered for narrations emphasizing simplicity, sincerity, and social justice in Islam. His bold character and proximity to the Prophet made him a distinctive and respected figure, and his narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Hudhaifa ibn al-Yaman (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) uniquely entrusted with knowledge of the signs of trials, tribulations, and future events (fitan). The Prophet shared with him the names of the hypocrites and secrets about events to come, making him a singular source for this type of narration. His hadiths are particularly important in chapters on eschatology and civil strife. He later served as a trusted governor of Mada'in under Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA).

Umm Salama (RA), whose full name was Hind bint Abi Umayya, was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was among the early Muslims who emigrated to Abyssinia and later to Medina, enduring great hardship for her faith. Known for her wisdom and sound judgment, she narrated numerous hadiths and was frequently consulted on religious matters by companions and Tabi'in alike. Her narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections and are considered authoritative.

Jabir ibn Abdullah (RA) was an Ansari companion from Medina who became one of the most prolific narrators among the companions. He participated in numerous battles alongside the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and spent many years transmitting prophetic knowledge to the Tabi'in. Known for his reliability, sincerity, and dedication to the Sunnah, his narrations cover a wide range of topics including worship, commerce, and the Prophet's Farewell Pilgrimage. He is among the most frequently cited companions in all major hadith literature.

Sa'id ibn Jubayr was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar and student of Ibn Abbas (RA). He was renowned for his deep knowledge of Quranic exegesis and Islamic law, and he transmitted many narrations from the companions with great care and accuracy. Known for his courage and piety, he ultimately died as a martyr at the hands of the tyrant al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, refusing to renounce his principles. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as one of the most reliable transmitters of his generation.

Abu Bakra (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam during the siege of Ta'if and was freed from slavery upon his acceptance of the faith. He settled in Basra after the Prophet's death and became an important source of prophetic traditions for the Muslim community there. He narrated numerous hadiths on various aspects of Islamic practice and belief, and his narrations are cited frequently in the major collections. He was known for his reliability and sincere commitment to the Sunnah.

Umm Salamah (RA) was one of the Mothers of the Believers and a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) of great wisdom and noble character. She was among the earliest Muslims and one of those who emigrated to Abyssinia before the Hijra. She narrated many hadiths and was known for her careful preservation of prophetic teachings, particularly on matters relating to women's religious practice, family life, and the Prophet's private conduct. Her narrations are valued across all major hadith collections.

Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) was the second Caliph of Islam and one of the most influential companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). His conversion to Islam was a turning point for the early Muslim community, and his caliphate is regarded as a model of just governance. Known for his unwavering commitment to the Sunnah, he narrated many hadiths and his juristic opinions are foundational references across all schools of Islamic law. His narrations are found in all the major hadith collections.

Masruq ibn al-Ajda was a Tabi'i scholar of Kufa who was a devoted student of major companions including Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) and Aisha (RA). He was highly regarded for his knowledge of Islamic law, his scrupulous approach to hadith narration, and his personal piety. He transmitted many narrations from the companions and is considered a reliable and authoritative link in the chains of transmission. Scholars praised him for his precision and integrity, and his narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections.

Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) was the second Caliph of Islam and a companion of the highest rank and distinction. He was among the closest associates of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), whose daughter Hafsa (RA) became a wife of the Prophet. His conversion strengthened the early Muslim community. He narrated hadiths that are foundational to Islamic scholarship and legal thought, and his understanding of the Sunnah deeply influenced the development of Islamic jurisprudence across all subsequent generations of scholars.

Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas (RA) was one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and was among the earliest Muslims, having embraced Islam as a young man. He was a skilled military commander who led the Muslim forces to victory at the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, opening Persia to Islam. He narrated numerous hadiths covering faith, prayer, and prophetic biography, and is cited in the major hadith collections as a trustworthy companion of the highest distinction.

Abu Wail Shaqiq ibn Salama al-Asadi was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) from Kufa who lived to approximately 82 AH. Though he missed seeing the Prophet ﷺ, he narrated hadiths from major Companions including Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, Ali ibn Abi Talib, Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman, and Abu Musa al-Ash'ari. He was among the most reliable and trusted transmitters in Kufa and a major link in the isnad chains connecting the Companions to the next generation. Scholars of hadith relied on his transmission extensively in the Six Major Collections.

Muhammad ibn Shihab al-Zuhri was one of the most important Tabi'i scholars and hadith transmitters in Islamic history. He was among the first to systematically collect and organize hadiths at the encouragement of Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz, playing a foundational role in the formal preservation of the Sunnah. He narrated from numerous companions and Tabi'in and is considered a cornerstone figure in the science of hadith. His narrations appear extensively throughout all major hadith collections and are regarded as among the most reliable.

Imran ibn Husain (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his deep piety, asceticism, and spiritual experiences. He narrated numerous hadiths and was greatly respected by the companions and Tabi'in for his knowledge of prophetic traditions and his closeness to the Prophet. He settled in Basra and became an important religious teacher in that region. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a reliable and distinguished narrator.

Abu Qatada al-Ansari (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from Medina who participated in many military expeditions and was known for his bravery and devotion. He narrated numerous hadiths covering various aspects of Islamic practice, including prayer, ritual purity, and the conduct of warfare. He was particularly active in transmitting prophetic knowledge after the Prophet's death, and his narrations are found in the major hadith collections where he is regarded as a reliable and sincere narrator.

Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) was the second Caliph of Islam, a companion of exceptional rank, and a close advisor to the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). His conversion to Islam significantly strengthened the early Muslim community, and his caliphate became synonymous with justice, piety, and the expansion of the Islamic state. He narrated hadiths that scholars across all generations have relied upon, and his understanding of the Sunnah and his juristic opinions are foundational to classical Islamic legal thought and governance.

Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was a Companion from the Ansar of Madinah who participated in eighteen military expeditions with the Prophet ﷺ. Initially too young to join the Battle of Badr, he later participated in all major campaigns. He narrated approximately 305 hadiths and settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death, where he became a key transmitter of prophetic traditions in Iraq. His reports cover prayer, fasting, funeral rites, and other pillars of Islamic practice.

Sahl ibn Sa'd al-Sa'idi (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his long life and his critical role in transmitting prophetic traditions to the Tabi'in. He was reportedly among the last surviving companions in Medina, which made him an essential direct link for scholars seeking authentic narrations from the prophetic era. His reports cover the Prophet's worship, character, and sayings, and are found throughout the major hadith collections where he is regarded as trustworthy.

Jabir ibn Abdullah (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina and one of the most prolific narrators among the companions, transmitting over 1,500 hadiths. He accompanied the Prophet on many expeditions and lived long enough to transmit prophetic knowledge to major Tabi'i scholars. His narrations are found throughout the major hadith collections and cover a wide range of religious topics including worship, transactions, and detailed accounts from the Prophet's life.

Urwa ibn al-Zubayr was a leading Tabi'i scholar of Medina, the son of the companion al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam (RA), and a nephew of Aisha (RA). He served as one of the primary transmitters of Aisha's narrations and is counted among the Seven Jurists of Medina, a group whose scholarly opinions shaped the foundations of early Islamic jurisprudence. His narrations appear extensively in all major hadith collections and he is regarded as one of the most reliable and authoritative Tabi'i transmitters.

Anas ibn Malik (RA) was the personal servant of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ for ten years, beginning when he was ten years old when his mother brought him to serve the Prophet in Madinah. He is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting over 2,200 traditions. The Prophet ﷺ made supplication for him: 'O Allah, give him wealth and children and bless him therein.' He lived to approximately 93 AH, becoming the last surviving major Companion in Basra, where he narrated to thousands of students. His long life and personal proximity to the Prophet made him an irreplaceable source of prophetic knowledge.

Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam in Medina and participated in numerous military campaigns in the early years of Islam. He narrated a significant number of hadiths on various topics including prayer, the funeral rites, and events from the Prophet's life and character. He is considered a reliable narrator whose transmissions are well-attested in the major hadith collections, and he continued to transmit prophetic knowledge well into the era of the Tabi'in.

Adi ibn Hatim al-Ta'i (RA) was initially a Christian Arab from the tribe of Tayy before embracing Islam around 9 AH. He was the son of the legendary Arab chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i, famous for his generosity. After accepting Islam, Adi ibn Hatim became a devoted Companion and narrated approximately 66 hadiths from the Prophet ﷺ. He participated in the early Islamic conquests and lived to approximately 68 AH, settling in Kufa. He was known for his loyalty to Ali (RA) during the period of civil strife and remained a respected elder figure in Iraq.

Muadh ibn Jabal (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) singled out as the most knowledgeable of the companions in matters of the lawful and the unlawful. The Prophet sent him to Yemen as a teacher and judge, demonstrating great confidence in his scholarship and character. He narrated hadiths on a wide range of topics and is regarded as one of the most learned and reliable companions in both religious knowledge and practical judgment. His narrations are found in all major hadith collections.

Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), raised in his household from a young age and among the very first to accept Islam. He served as the fourth Caliph of Islam and was renowned for his courage, deep piety, and vast Islamic scholarship. He narrated numerous hadiths and his scholarly legacy has deeply influenced Islamic jurisprudence and spirituality across all subsequent generations. His narrations are considered highly authoritative in all major schools of Islamic thought.

Usama ibn Zaid (RA) was the son of Zaid ibn Haritha (RA), the freed companion, and was deeply beloved by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), who called him 'my beloved, son of my beloved.' He was appointed by the Prophet to lead a military expedition at a young age, demonstrating the Prophet's confidence in him. He narrated several important hadiths and is cited in the major hadith collections. His unique closeness to the Prophet's household gave his narrations a particular significance and authority.

Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam in his youth and participated in many military expeditions in the early history of Islam. He narrated numerous hadiths on a wide range of topics including prayer, the funeral rites, descriptions of the Prophet, and events from his life. He lived to an old age and continued transmitting prophetic knowledge to the Tabi'in. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a reliable and sincere narrator.

Buraydah ibn al-Hasib al-Aslami (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who led his entire tribe to embrace Islam when he himself converted. He participated in many military expeditions and later settled in Khurasan, where he became an important transmitter of prophetic knowledge in that region. He narrated numerous hadiths covering many aspects of Islamic worship and conduct. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is considered a reliable and trustworthy narrator.

Samurah ibn Jundub (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who narrated a number of prophetic traditions and later served as a governor of Basra. He is known in hadith literature for transmitting several narrations related to Quranic recitation, dream interpretation, and religious observances. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections. Despite some scholarly discussion about a few of his transmissions, he is generally regarded as an accepted narrator from among the companions.

Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (RA), whose full name was Uqba ibn Amr al-Ansari al-Badri, was from the Ansar of Madinah and was present at the Second Pledge of Aqabah. He participated in the Battle of Badr — a distinction that earned him the epithet 'al-Badri.' He narrated approximately 103 hadiths from the Prophet ﷺ covering prayer, charity, the Day of Judgment, and the Prophet's character. He later settled in Kufa where he served as an important early authority on hadith and Islamic rulings. He died during the caliphate of Muawiya.

This narrator refers to Urwa ibn al-Zubayr, the father of Hisham ibn Urwa, as identified in certain hadith chains where Hisham narrates on his father's authority. Urwa was a leading Tabi'i scholar of Medina, a nephew of Aisha (RA), and one of the Seven Jurists of Medina. He was an authoritative and highly reliable transmitter of prophetic narrations who played a central role in preserving the knowledge of the Prophet's household. His narrations transmitted through his son Hisham form an important and widely attested chain found throughout the major hadith collections.

Amr ibn Shu'ayb narrated on the authority of his father Shu'ayb ibn Muhammad, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (RA), a companion of the Prophet (peace be upon him). This chain — Amr from his father from his grandfather — is a recognized and frequently discussed isnad in hadith criticism, accepted by many scholars as reliable while others examined its continuity carefully. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and contain important legal rulings and prophetic guidance.

Mujahid ibn Jabr was a leading Tabi'i scholar and one of the most important authorities on Quranic tafsir. He was a devoted student of Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA) and reportedly recited the entire Quran to him multiple times, pausing at each verse to ask about its meaning and context. His tafsir narrations are among the most foundational references in classical Quranic scholarship. He also narrated hadiths from companions and is considered a reliable and authoritative transmitter in the chains of hadith.

Qatada ibn Di'ama was a prominent Tabi'i scholar of Basra renowned for his extraordinary memory and expertise in Quranic exegesis and hadith. He narrated from many companions and is considered a major figure in the transmission of both tafsir and prophetic traditions. Despite being blind from birth, he excelled in scholarship and memorization beyond most of his peers. He is regarded as one of the most knowledgeable and reliable narrators of his generation and his narrations appear extensively in the major hadith collections.

Jarir ibn Abdullah al-Bajali (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam and was known for his noble character and dignified presence. The Prophet reportedly remarked that Jarir resembled the prophets of the Bani Isra'il. He narrated several hadiths preserved in the major collections, covering topics relating to faith, the conduct of a Muslim, and various aspects of Islamic practice. He was respected by the companions and Tabi'in for his sincerity and his commitment to the Sunnah.

Qais ibn Abi Hazim was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar of Kufa who narrated from a remarkable number of senior companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), including Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, and many others. He is considered one of the most prolific Tabi'i transmitters from the first generation of companions and is regarded as highly reliable by the scholars of hadith. His narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections and represent an important bridge between the companions and later scholarship.

Zaid ibn Thabit (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who served as one of his primary scribes, recording Quranic revelation and official correspondence. He was entrusted with the monumental task of compiling the Quran into a single volume during the caliphate of Abu Bakr (RA) and later oversaw the preparation of the standardized Uthmanic mushaf. He narrated numerous hadiths and was a leading scholarly authority in Medina on matters of Quranic science, recitation, and Islamic law.

Abu Sa'eed al-Khudri (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina who narrated hundreds of hadiths. He is particularly known for narrations on eschatology, the signs of the Last Day, and matters of purification and prayer. His dedication to preserving and transmitting the Sunnah made him one of the most cited companions in the hadith literature, and his narrations are found extensively in all major collections where they are regarded as highly authentic.

Abu Hurayrah (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and the single most prolific narrator of hadith, with over 5,000 narrations attributed to him. He devoted himself entirely to learning from the Prophet, sacrificing comfort and worldly pursuits to remain in his company. After the Prophet's death, he continued to teach and transmit this knowledge for decades, becoming an essential and irreplaceable source for Islamic tradition. His narrations are foundational across all major hadith collections.

Umm Salamah (RA) was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was among the earliest Muslims and emigrated twice — first to Abyssinia and then to Medina — enduring hardship for her faith. Known for her wisdom and intelligence, she was frequently consulted on religious matters. She narrated numerous hadiths and continued to be a scholarly authority after the Prophet's death. Her narrations are cited in all major hadith collections and are particularly valued on matters of worship and household conduct.

Amr ibn Shu'ayb narrated from his father Shu'ayb ibn Muhammad, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (RA), a companion of the Prophet (peace be upon him) and one of the first to systematically write down hadith. This three-generation chain is accepted by many hadith scholars as reliable, though it has been the subject of scholarly analysis regarding its continuity. Narrations through this chain appear in the major hadith collections and contain valuable religious rulings and prophetic guidance.

name_en: Abu Huraira and Zaid bin Khalid
bio_short_en: This entry refers to narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by two companions: Abu Huraira (RA), the most prolific hadith narrator in Islamic history, and Zaid ibn Khalid al-Juhani (RA), an Ansari companion known for his piety, generosity, and devotion. Both were companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), and their joint narrations typically appear in the context of a shared experience or their both hearing the same prophetic statement. Their combined transmission adds strength to the authenticity of the narrations they share.

Abu Burda ibn Abi Musa al-Ashari was a Tabi'i scholar and the son of the companion Abu Musa al-Ashari (RA). He narrated hadiths from his father and from other companions, and is regarded as a reliable transmitter. He served as a judge in Kufa and was respected in the community for his knowledge, integrity, and sound legal judgment. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and provide an important link in the chains of transmission from the companions to subsequent generations of scholars.

Maimuna bint al-Harith (RA) was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was known for her piety, generosity, and deep devotion to worship. She narrated a number of hadiths relating to the Prophet's personal conduct, prayer, and purification, offering valuable insight into his private life and religious practice. She was respected among the companions and Tabi'in as a reliable narrator and an authoritative source of knowledge about the Prophet's household.

Sahl ibn Sa'd al-Sa'idi (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who was among the last surviving companions in Medina. His extraordinary longevity allowed him to transmit prophetic knowledge directly to Tabi'i scholars who had no personal experience of the Prophet's era, making him an invaluable bridge to the prophetic tradition. He narrated hadiths on topics ranging from worship to the Prophet's character and miracles, and his narrations are found in all the major hadith collections.

This refers to Abdullah ibn Umar (RA), identified as the father of Salim in certain isnad references. Abdullah ibn Umar was one of the most prolific and scrupulous narrator-companions, known for his extreme care in adhering to the Prophet's Sunnah in every aspect of his life. His narrations transmitted through his son Salim represent one of the most reliable and well-documented chains in hadith literature, appearing throughout the major collections and carrying great weight among scholars of the hadith sciences.

Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas (RA) was one of the earliest Muslims and one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He was a military leader of great distinction who commanded the Muslim forces at the pivotal Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, leading to the conquest of Persia. He narrated numerous hadiths and is cited across the major collections. His early acceptance of Islam and his close relationship with the Prophet made him a highly authoritative and respected narrator.

Abu Juhaifa Wahb ibn Abdullah (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who narrated several valuable hadiths. He is particularly well known for a narration describing the physical appearance of the Prophet in vivid detail, which is among the most widely cited descriptions in the hadith literature. He settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death and became an important transmitter of prophetic knowledge to the scholars there. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and are considered reliable.

Salim ibn Abdullah ibn Umar was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and the son of the companion Abdullah ibn Umar (RA). He was recognized as one of the Seven Jurists of Medina, a select group whose scholarly depth and influence shaped the foundations of early Islamic jurisprudence. He transmitted many narrations from his father, one of the most scrupulous companion narrators, and is himself regarded as highly reliable. His narrations appear extensively in the major hadith collections and he is counted among the most authoritative Tabi'i scholars.

Al-Aswad ibn Yazid al-Nakha'i was a prominent Tabi'i scholar of Kufa and a devoted student of major companions including Aisha (RA) and Abdullah ibn Masud (RA). He was a nephew of the companion Alqama ibn Qais and was known for his strict asceticism and deep personal piety. He narrated numerous hadiths and is regarded as a highly reliable transmitter by the scholars of hadith. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and he is considered an authoritative and trustworthy link in the chains of transmission.

Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA) was among the earliest Muslims and one of the most principled and outspoken companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He was known for his rejection of worldly wealth, his deep spirituality, and his fearless advocacy for justice regardless of personal consequence. He narrated hadiths on topics ranging from faith and prayer to social ethics, and is regarded as a sincere and reliable narrator. His narrations are found throughout the major hadith collections and he is revered as a model of conviction.

Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his knowledge of Quranic recitation, Islamic law, and military arts. He served as the governor of Egypt during the caliphate of Muawiyah and was highly respected for his scholarship, piety, and administrative ability. He narrated numerous hadiths covering topics such as prayer, charity, and proper conduct. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections where he is regarded as a trustworthy and reliable narrator.

Ikrima was a Tabi'i scholar and the freed slave of Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), through whom he transmitted a large body of hadiths and tafsir narrations. He was considered one of the most knowledgeable scholars of Quranic exegesis of his generation and traveled widely to transmit and gather knowledge. His narrations appear in all the major hadith collections. Despite some scholarly discussion about his character in early biographical literature, the majority of hadith scholars accepted him as a reliable transmitter of prophetic traditions.

Ubada ibn al-Samit (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the respected leaders of the Ansar. He was among those who took the first and second pledges of Aqabah, demonstrating his early and committed support for Islam. He participated in major military campaigns and later served as a teacher and judge in the Levant. His narrations cover topics including the fundamentals of faith and Islamic practice, and they are found in the major hadith collections.

Jubayr ibn Mut'im (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam after initially opposing it. He was known for his expertise in Arab genealogy and his knowledge of religious matters. He narrated a number of hadiths relating to the Quran, prayer, and various aspects of Islamic practice and belief. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is considered a reliable narrator whose transmissions provide valuable insight into the prophetic era.

Thawban (RA) was a freed slave of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who became so deeply devoted to him that he could not bear to be apart from him. After the Prophet's death, he settled in Syria and continued to transmit prophetic knowledge to subsequent generations. He narrated numerous hadiths and is particularly known for narrations about the latter times, the virtues of certain religious practices, and the importance of steadfastness in worship. His narrations are found throughout the major hadith collections.

Imran ibn Husayn (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his exceptional piety, spiritual experiences, and commitment to transmitting prophetic knowledge. He settled in Basra and became one of its most important religious teachers, guiding the Muslim community there for many years. He narrated numerous hadiths on topics including prayer, worship, and the Prophet's character and conduct. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a reliable and distinguished narrator.

Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who dedicated himself wholeheartedly to preserving and transmitting the Sunnah after the Prophet's death. He narrated hundreds of hadiths on topics including worship, legal rulings, eschatology, and the signs of the Last Day, and was one of the most active and devoted narrators among the companions. His narrations are found extensively in all the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a highly trustworthy and authoritative narrator of prophetic tradition.

Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (RA) was among the earliest Muslims and suffered some of the most severe persecution at the hands of the Quraysh for his refusal to renounce Islam. He was a blacksmith by trade and endured brutal torture, yet remained steadfast in his faith. He narrated a number of hadiths preserved in the major collections, including narrations about the early hardships of the Muslim community and various aspects of Islamic practice. He is revered as a towering example of perseverance and sincerity in the path of faith.

Amr is a common name among early Muslim narrators, borne by several Companions and Successors. The most prominent include Amr ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him), the renowned Companion, military commander, and conqueror of Egypt, and Amr ibn Dinar, a leading Successor scholar of Mecca. Without additional context, this entry refers to one of several narrators of this name who transmitted hadith in the early Islamic tradition.

Al-Mughira bin Shu'ba (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. He was a skilled diplomat and military leader, serving as governor of Basra and Kufa during the caliphates of Umar and Uthman. He narrated numerous hadiths and was known for his intelligence and political acumen. He passed away around 50 AH.

Abu Salama ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was a leading Successor scholar of Medina and one of the seven great jurists of the city. He was the son of the Companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf (may Allah be pleased with him). Abu Salama transmitted extensively from Aisha, Abu Huraira, and other Companions, making him a key link in numerous hadith chains. He was highly regarded for his piety and legal knowledge.

Rafi bin Khadij (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in several battles and is known for narrating hadiths on agricultural contracts, particularly the prohibition of certain share-cropping arrangements. He was a reliable narrator whose reports are found in the major hadith collections. He lived until approximately 74 AH.

Abu Is-haq al-Sabi'i (Amr ibn Abdullah al-Hamdani) was a prominent Successor scholar from Kufa who lived through the era of the senior Companions. He narrated from numerous Companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, al-Bara ibn Azib, and Zaid ibn Arqam (may Allah be pleased with them). He was considered a highly reliable narrator in his earlier years and transmitted a large number of hadiths. He passed away around 127 AH.

Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him) was a distinguished Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in twelve battles alongside the Prophet and narrated over one thousand hadiths, making him among the most prolific Companion narrators. He was particularly known for his hadiths on Islamic jurisprudence, eschatology, and ethical conduct. He passed away in Medina around 74 AH.

Tawus ibn Kaysan al-Yamani was a leading Successor scholar and jurist from Yemen. He narrated from numerous Companions including Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with them), and was considered among the most learned men of his generation. He was known for his asceticism, refusal of gifts from rulers, and independent legal opinions. He passed away in Mecca around 106 AH during the Hajj season.

Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca. He served as a scribe of the Quranic revelation and later became the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate, ruling for nearly twenty years. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and is regarded as a Companion within Ahl us-Sunnah, though his political role remains a subject of historical discussion. He passed away in 60 AH.

Zaid bin Khalid al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the tribe of Juhayna. He participated in the Battle of Hudaybiyyah and is particularly known for narrating hadiths related to found property (luqata), purification, and prayer. He settled in Medina and later Kufa, where he transmitted hadith until his passing around 68 AH. His reports appear in all major hadith collections.

Abdullah bin Az-Zubair (may Allah be pleased with him) was the son of the Companion Al-Zubair ibn al-Awwam and the grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. He was the first child born to the Muslim emigrants (Muhajirun) in Medina after the Hijra, and his birth was considered a blessed sign for the community. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from senior Companions, and later became the Caliph in Mecca. He was martyred in 73 AH.

An-Nu'man bin Bashir (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad whose father Bashir ibn Sa'd was also a Companion. He was brought to the Prophet as an infant, making him among the youngest Companions. He later served as governor of Kufa and Homs under Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan. He is renowned for narrating the famous hadith on the halal and haram being clear and the importance of avoiding doubtful matters. He was martyred around 65 AH.

Abu Hazim al-Ashja'i (Salmaan, the freed slave of Azda) was a Successor who transmitted hadiths from several Companions. A second narrator of the same name, Abu Hazim ibn Dinar al-Araj, was a notable Medinian Successor scholar who narrated extensively from Abu Huraira and Sahl ibn Sa'd. Both were reliable transmitters whose reports appear in the Sahihayn and Sunan collections. The name Abu Hazim was borne by several trustworthy scholars of the early generations.

Salama bin Al-Akwa (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad renowned for his exceptional speed and skill in foot-racing. He participated in numerous battles, including Hudaybiyyah, Khaybar, and Hunayn, and gave his pledge of allegiance under the tree at Hudaybiyyah. He narrated many hadiths related to warfare, hunting, and the Prophet's conduct. In his later years he settled in the Rabadha region and passed away around 74 AH.

Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with him) was the most prolific narrator of hadith among all the Companions of the Prophet Muhammad, transmitting over five thousand reports. He embraced Islam in 7 AH and remained closely attached to the Prophet, dedicating himself to memorizing his sayings. He served as governor of Bahrain and later settled in Medina, where he taught thousands of students. He passed away around 57-59 AH. This entry is a variant spelling of the same Companion.

Abu Bakrah Nufay ibn al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who came to Islam during the Siege of Ta'if, descending from the fortress to join the Muslims. He settled in Basra, where he became one of the leading Companions in that region and a respected transmitter of hadith. He is well known for narrating the Prophet's prohibition against fighting in civil strife. He passed away in Basra around 51-52 AH.

Abdullah ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him) was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the greatest scholars among the Companions. The Prophet supplicated for him, asking Allah to grant him deep understanding of religion and knowledge of the Quran's interpretation. He became the foremost authority on Quranic exegesis (tafsir) and Islamic jurisprudence, earning the title Hibr al-Umma (Scholar of the Nation) and Turjuman al-Quran (Interpreter of the Quran). He narrated over one thousand six hundred hadiths and passed away in Ta'if around 68 AH.

Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri (Muhammad ibn Muslim ibn Shihab) was one of the most important Successor scholars in Islamic history, considered a master of hadith science and one of the earliest systematic collectors of the Prophet's traditions. He narrated from numerous Companions including Anas ibn Malik and Sahl ibn Sa'd, and served as a teacher to Imam Malik and many other leading scholars. He was among the first to formally compile hadiths on the instruction of Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz. He passed away around 124 AH.

Qatadah ibn Di'ama al-Sadusi was a prominent Successor scholar from Basra, renowned as a memorizer of the Quran and an authority in hadith and Quranic exegesis. Despite being blind from birth, he possessed an extraordinary memory and narrated from Anas ibn Malik, Abdullah ibn Sarjas, and other Companions. He was a student of al-Hasan al-Basri and a leading representative of the Basran scholarly tradition. He passed away around 117 AH.

Abu Uthman al-Nahdi (Abd al-Rahman ibn Mall) was a senior Successor scholar who lived an exceptionally long life, reportedly reaching 130 years of age. He narrated from several Companions including Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, Salman al-Farsi, and Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with them). He was based in Basra and was considered a highly reliable and trustworthy narrator. He passed away around 95-100 AH.

Salama is a name shared by several early Muslim narrators. The most notable is Salama bin Al-Akwa (may Allah be pleased with him), the Companion renowned for his bravery and speed in battle. Another is Salama ibn Kuhail, a Successor scholar from Kufa who narrated from several Companions and Successors and was considered reliable by hadith critics. Without further context, this entry refers to one of the several narrators named Salama in the early Islamic tradition.

As-Sa'ib ibn Yazid (RA) was a Companion who, according to his own account, was brought before the Prophet ﷺ as a young child and the Prophet ﷺ passed his hand over his head in blessing. He participated in Hajj with the Prophet ﷺ at the age of seven during the Farewell Pilgrimage. He went on to serve in the administration during the caliphate of Umar (RA) and later Uthman (RA). He narrated hadiths relating to the Prophet's physical description, conduct, and acts of worship. He lived to approximately 91 AH and was one of the last Companions to survive in Madinah.

Um Atiyya al-Ansariyyah (Nusayba bint al-Harith, may Allah be pleased with her) was a female Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Medina. She participated in seven military expeditions, providing medical care and support to the wounded. She narrated hadiths related to the washing of the deceased, funeral rites, and women's religious practice, and her reports are particularly important in Islamic jurisprudence. She is among the most cited female Companions in the hadith literature.

Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was a Companion from the Ansar of Madinah who participated in eighteen military expeditions with the Prophet ﷺ. Initially too young to join the Battle of Badr, he later participated in all major campaigns. He narrated approximately 305 hadiths and settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death, where he became a key transmitter of prophetic traditions in Iraq. His reports cover prayer, fasting, funeral rites, and other pillars of Islamic practice.

Ibn Abi Mulaika (Abdullah ibn Ubaydullah ibn Abi Mulaika al-Tamimi) was a Successor scholar and judge from Mecca who served as a judge during the caliphate of Ibn al-Zubair. He narrated extensively from Aisha, Ibn Abbas, and other senior Companions (may Allah be pleased with them), and was considered a highly reliable narrator by hadith scholars. He is known for transmitting many hadiths on Islamic law, ethics, and the conduct of the Prophet. He passed away around 117 AH.

Samurah ibn Jundab (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a number of hadiths. He served as governor of Basra under Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan and was known as an administrator. He narrated hadiths on prayer, commercial transactions, and prophetic supplications. His reports appear in the Sunan collections. This entry may overlap with Samurah bin Jundab listed separately; both refer to the same Companion.

Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (Uqba ibn Amr al-Badri, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in the Battle of Badr, which gave him his well-known epithet. He narrated numerous hadiths and later settled in Kufa, where he became a respected religious authority. He is known for transmitting the famous hadith on the connection between the prayer and charity (zakat) and is recognized as a reliable narrator in all major collections.

Al-Miswar bin Makhrama (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad, born in Mecca two years before the Hijra. He was the nephew of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf and narrated hadiths from the Prophet, Abu Bakr, and Umar (may Allah be pleased with them). He is known for his narrations related to the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and the Prophet's conduct in diplomacy. He died at the time of Ibn al-Zubair's siege of Mecca around 64 AH.

Zaid bin Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina who participated in seventeen military expeditions. He is known for narrating the famous hadith of Ghadir Khumm in which the Prophet emphasized the importance of the Quran and his household. He transmitted many hadiths on theology, prayer, and the Prophet's virtues, and settled in Kufa where he was held in high esteem. He passed away around 65-68 AH.

Sa'id bin Al-Musaiyab was one of the seven great jurists of Medina and the foremost Successor scholar of his generation. His father and grandfather were Companions of the Prophet. He narrated extensively from Abu Huraira, whose daughter he married, as well as from Umar, Uthman, Ali, and Aisha (may Allah be pleased with them). He was renowned for his legal acumen, his refusal to accept gifts from rulers, and his profound piety. He passed away around 94 AH.

Abu Sa'eed is likely a variant reference to Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him), the prolific Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He narrated over one thousand hadiths and was a key transmitter of prophetic traditions on jurisprudence, eschatology, and ethics. Alternatively, this may refer to another narrator sharing this kunya. His reports are found in all major hadith collections and are highly relied upon by Islamic scholars.

Abu Umamah al-Bahili (Sudayy ibn Ajlan, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who was the last surviving Companion in Syria. He narrated a large number of hadiths and was renowned for his frequent recitation of the Quran. He settled in Syria and became a leading religious authority there. He is known for hadiths encouraging the recitation of Surah al-Baqarah to ward off evil, and for numerous narrations on worship and virtue. He passed away around 86-88 AH.

Abu Musa al-Ash'ari (Abdullah ibn Qays, may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Yemen. He was a renowned reciter of the Quran, and the Prophet praised his beautiful voice, saying it was like a flute of the family of David. He served as governor of Basra and Kufa and played a significant diplomatic role. He narrated many hadiths on prayer, recitation, and Islamic conduct. He passed away around 42-52 AH.

Abu al-Darda (Uwaymir ibn Zayd, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina, known for his wisdom, asceticism, and deep knowledge of religion. He embraced Islam after the Battle of Badr and became one of the learned scholars among the Companions. He served as a judge in Damascus and was sent by Caliph Uthman to teach the people of Syria. He narrated many hadiths on wisdom, piety, and spiritual conduct. He passed away around 32 AH.

Asma bint Abi Bakr (RA) was the elder sister of Aisha (RA) and the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA). She played a crucial role in the Prophet's migration to Madinah by carrying food to the Prophet ﷺ and her father as they hid in the Cave of Thawr. She is known as 'Dhat al-Nitaqayn' — the Lady of the Two Girdles — because she tore her girdle to tie the provisions for the Hijra. She narrated approximately 58 hadiths and lived to 100 years of age, dying shortly after the death of her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr in 73 AH.

Abu Umamah al-Bahili (Sudayy ibn Ajlan, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who became the last surviving Companion in the Levant region. He narrated a significant number of hadiths and was well known for his dedication to Quranic recitation and religious teaching in Syria. He is cited in the Sunan and Musnad collections for hadiths on worship, ethics, and the virtues of specific deeds. He passed away around 86-88 AH. This entry is a variant of AbuUmamah.

Al-Hasan al-Basri (al-Hasan ibn Abi al-Hasan Yasar) was one of the greatest Successor scholars and spiritual figures in Islamic history. Born in Medina in 21 AH, he narrated from numerous Companions including Anas ibn Malik, Abu Huraira, and Aisha (may Allah be pleased with them). He became the leading scholar of Basra, renowned for his eloquence, profound piety, and asceticism. He was a major transmitter of hadiths and a foundational figure in Islamic spirituality and ethics. He passed away in 110 AH.

Uqbah bin Amir al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who served as governor of Egypt under Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan. He was skilled in archery and the Prophet encouraged his practice of it. He narrated numerous hadiths on prayer, charity, and the virtues of Islamic practice, and is known for preserving traditions related to archery and horsemanship. He passed away in Egypt around 58 AH. This entry may overlap with Uqba bin Amir listed separately.

Amr bin Dinar al-Makki was a prominent Successor scholar and jurist from Mecca. He narrated from the Companions Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, Jabir ibn Abdullah, and others (may Allah be pleased with them), and was one of the principal teachers of Imam al-Shafi'i's teacher, Ibn Uyayna. He was considered among the most reliable and widely respected narrators of his generation. His hadiths appear prominently in the Sahihayn and the Sunan collections. He passed away around 126 AH.

Ibn Juraij (Abd al-Malik ibn Abd al-Aziz ibn Juraij) was a major Successor scholar from Mecca and one of the early systematizers of hadith collection. He was among the first scholars to compile hadiths in written form and narrated from Ata ibn Abi Rabah, Amr ibn Dinar, and Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri among others. He is considered a foundational figure in the development of Islamic jurisprudence in the Meccan school. He passed away around 150 AH.

Uqba ibn Amir al-Juhani (RA) was a Companion who embraced Islam in the early Medinan period and participated in numerous expeditions. He is known for his expertise in archery — the Prophet ﷺ encouraged Muslims to learn archery and Uqba was particularly skilled. He served as the governor of Egypt during the caliphate of Muawiya (RA) from 44–47 AH. He narrated approximately 55 hadiths relating to prayer, archery, conduct, and the virtues of reciting the Quran. He died in Egypt around 58 AH and is buried there.

Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam as a youth in Medina and participated in many military campaigns. He narrated numerous hadiths on topics including prayer, the funeral rites, the Quran, and the Prophet's physical description and conduct. He lived to a considerable age and continued transmitting prophetic knowledge to the scholars of the next generation. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections where he is regarded as a reliable and sincere narrator.

Aisha bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her) was the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the greatest scholars of Islam. She narrated over two thousand hadiths and was a primary source of knowledge about the Prophet's private life, worship, and character. After the Prophet's passing, she became a leading teacher and jurist, with Companions and Successors traveling to consult her on religious matters. She passed away in Medina in 58 AH at approximately sixty-six years of age.

Al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah (may Allah be pleased with him) was a distinguished Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. He was a gifted diplomat and military commander who served as governor of Basra and Kufa. He narrated numerous hadiths and was known for his intelligence and political skill. He is cited in the major hadith collections for reports on prayer, the Sunnah, and the conduct of the Prophet. He passed away around 50 AH. This is a variant spelling of Al-Mughira bin Shu'ba.

An-Nu'man ibn Bashir (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the youngest among them. His father Bashir ibn Sa'd was a Companion who participated in the Battle of Badr, and he was brought to the Prophet as an infant. He later served as governor of Kufa and Homs. He is most famous for narrating the foundational hadith: 'The halal is clear and the haram is clear, and between them are doubtful matters.' He was martyred around 65 AH. This is a variant spelling of An-Nu'man bin Bashir.

Wa'il ibn Hujr (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from a noble Hadrami family in Yemen. He was received warmly by the Prophet, who gave him land and sent Companions to accompany him back to Yemen. He narrated hadiths primarily on the physical description of the Prophet's prayer, including the placement of hands and the raising of the finger in tashahhud, which are foundational for Islamic prayer practice. He settled in Kufa and his descendants became notable scholars.

Ka'b bin Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was a renowned Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina and one of the three Companions who remained behind from the Battle of Tabuk. His account of repentance and Allah's acceptance, narrated at length in Sahih al-Bukhari, is one of the most detailed stories in the hadith literature. He was also a poet who defended the Prophet through verse. He narrated many hadiths and passed away around 50 AH.

Humaid al-Tawil (Humaid ibn Abi Humaid) was a Successor scholar from Basra who narrated extensively from Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him), to whom he had direct access for many years. He is among the most important transmitters of Anas's hadiths and his narrations appear frequently in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim. He was considered reliable and trustworthy by hadith scholars. He passed away around 142-143 AH.

Abdullah bin Dinar was a Successor scholar from Medina who was a freed slave (mawla) of Abdullah ibn Umar (may Allah be pleased with him). He narrated extensively from Ibn Umar, whose household he served, and his hadiths appear prominently in the Sahihayn. He was considered a highly reliable narrator by the scholars of hadith criticism and was a key transmitter of Medinan traditions. He passed away around 127 AH.

name_en: Abu Sa'id Al-Khudri and Abu Huraira
bio_short_en: This entry refers to hadiths narrated jointly or within the same chain by two prominent Companions: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with them both). Abu Sa'id was an Ansari Companion who narrated over one thousand hadiths, while Abu Huraira was the most prolific Companion narrator with over five thousand hadiths. When their names appear together, the hadith typically has a combined or parallel chain from both authorities, strengthening its authenticity.

Asma is a name shared by several female Companions and early Muslim women. The most prominent is Asma bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her), daughter of the first Caliph Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, who earned the title Dhat al-Nitaqayn (the one of the two belts) for her role during the Hijra. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from her father, and lived to nearly one hundred years. Another is Asma bint Yazid, a Companion who narrated from the Prophet on matters affecting women.

Asma bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad and the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first Caliph. She earned the renowned title Dhat al-Nitaqayn (She of the Two Belts) for splitting her belt to provide food for the Prophet and her father during their migration to Medina. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet and lived to nearly one hundred years, witnessing the era of her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubair. She passed away in 73 AH.

Jarir bin Abdullah al-Bajali (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam in 10 AH, near the end of the prophetic era. The Prophet praised his handsome appearance and his goodness. He was sent by the Prophet to destroy the idol Dhu al-Khalasa in Yemen. He settled in Kufa and narrated many hadiths, including the famous tradition on the obligation to advise and show sincerity to Muslim leaders. He passed away around 51-54 AH.

Abdullah bin Abi Qatada was a Successor scholar and the son of the Companion Abu Qatada al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him), one of the Prophet's horsemen known for his bravery in battle. Abdullah narrated hadiths from his father and other Companions, transmitting traditions related to prayer, fasting, and the conduct of the Prophet. His reports serve as an important link between the Companion generation and the Successor scholars.

Abdullah bin Mughaffal al-Muzani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the distinguished members of the Ahl al-Suffa, the poor Companions who lived in the mosque of Medina. He participated in the conquest of Mecca and was among those who carried the banner during battles. He narrated hadiths on prayer, purification, and Islamic etiquette, and later settled in Basra. He passed away around 57-60 AH.

Uqba bin Al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Mecca who embraced Islam at the conquest. He narrated a relatively small number of hadiths, among the most notable being a report related to the prohibition of intoxicants following an incident where a man claimed a certain woman had suckled him and the man he sought to marry her to. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are cited in matters of Islamic family law.

Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) was one of the earliest converts to Islam, embracing the faith while still a young man in Makkah. He was so close to the Prophet ﷺ that he was given permission to enter the Prophet's home without seeking permission and described as one who resembled the Prophet ﷺ most closely in his walk and conduct. He narrated 848 hadiths and was appointed by Umar (RA) as a teacher and judge in Kufa. He is particularly renowned for his Quranic recitation and is considered one of the four great authorities on the Quran among the Companions.

Jabir bin Abdullah al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Medina who participated in nineteen military expeditions. His father Abdullah ibn Amr was martyred at Uhud. Jabir narrated approximately one thousand five hundred hadiths and is known for detailed accounts of the Prophet's Farewell Pilgrimage, acts of worship, and daily conduct. He passed away in Medina around 73-78 AH and was the last surviving Companion in that city.

Jabir bin Samurah (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad, the son of Samurah ibn Jundab and nephew of Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas. He narrated approximately one hundred and forty hadiths and is known particularly for his narrations describing the physical appearance of the Prophet, accounts of Friday prayer, and the signs of the Hour. He settled in Kufa and was considered a reliable narrator. He passed away around 74 AH.

Salman al-Farsi (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who had journeyed from Persia in search of the true religion, passing through Christianity before reaching Islam. The Prophet said about him: 'Salman is from us, the People of the House.' He suggested digging the trench during the Battle of the Trench, an innovative military strategy that proved decisive. He served as governor of al-Mada'in and was known for his profound knowledge and asceticism. He passed away around 36 AH.

Ubadah ibn as-Samit (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina and one of the twelve representatives (nuqaba) selected at the Second Pledge of Aqaba. He participated in the Battle of Badr and transmitted important hadiths on the conditions of the shahada, the obligation of prayer, and the prohibition of usury. He served as a judge in Palestine and narrated hadiths that form foundational principles in Islamic jurisprudence. He passed away around 34 AH.

Buraidah ibn al-Husayb al-Aslami (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the tribe of Aslam. He embraced Islam when the Prophet passed through his territory during the Hijra to Medina. He narrated numerous hadiths on jihad, prayer, and the conduct of the Prophet, and his reports are found in all major hadith collections. He settled in Marw (in present-day Turkmenistan) where he became a religious authority and passed away around 62-63 AH.

Rafi ibn Khadij (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina who participated in numerous battles. He is particularly known for narrating hadiths related to agricultural share-cropping arrangements and the conditions under which they are permissible or prohibited. His reports are fundamental in Islamic commercial law. He lived until approximately 74 AH. This entry is a variant spelling of Rafi bin Khadij.

Samurah bin Jundab (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a number of hadiths on prayer, dreams, and prophetic conduct. He served as governor of Basra during the caliphate of Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan and was known as an administrator. He is cited in the major hadith collections for traditions including the Prophet's account of a long dream describing the states of various people in the afterlife. He passed away around 58-60 AH.

Abu Qilaba al-Jarmi (Abdullah ibn Zayd al-Azdi) was a prominent Successor scholar from Basra who narrated from Anas ibn Malik, Malik ibn al-Huwayrith, and other Companions. He was known for his strong adherence to the Sunnah and his caution in narrating hadiths. He eventually relocated to Syria to distance himself from the political turbulence of his era. He is cited in the Sahihayn and Sunan for hadiths on prayer, purification, and Islamic conduct. He passed away around 104-107 AH.

Hakim bin Hizam (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who had the distinction of being born inside the Ka'ba, making him the only person in history known to have been born in that sacred place. He was the nephew of Khadijah, the Prophet's first wife. He embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca and went on to perform extensive acts of charity and freeing of slaves. He narrated hadiths on commerce and generosity and passed away around 54-60 AH at a very advanced age.

Ibn Abu Mulaika (Abdullah ibn Ubaydullah ibn Abi Mulaika) was a Successor scholar from Mecca who served as a judge under Caliph Ibn al-Zubair and as a muezzin and prayer leader. He narrated from Aisha, Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and other senior Companions (may Allah be pleased with them), and was regarded as a highly reliable narrator. His hadiths appear prominently in the Sahihayn. He passed away around 117 AH. This entry is a variant spelling of Ibn Abi Mulaika.

Ibn Abi Aufa (Abdullah ibn Abi Aufa al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the Battle of Hudaybiyyah and numerous other expeditions. He was among the last Companions to pass away in Kufa, living to a very advanced age, and was considered the last surviving Companion in that city. He narrated hadiths on prayer, charity, and the Prophet's conduct in battle. He passed away around 86-87 AH.

Abdullah bin Abi Aufa al-Aslami (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the Battle of Hudaybiyyah and gave the pledge under the tree. He settled in Kufa, where he became the last surviving Companion in the city, living well into old age. He narrated hadiths on prayer, war, and the conduct of the Prophet, and his reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim. He passed away around 86-87 AH. This is a fuller version of the Ibn Abi Aufa entry.

Abdullah ibn Masud (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the most eminent Companions of the Prophet Muhammad and among the earliest to embrace Islam. He was the Prophet's close personal attendant, trusted to carry his sandals and prepare his bath. He was a master of Quranic recitation and is known for his own mushaf. He became the primary religious scholar and judge in Kufa, where thousands of students learned from him. He narrated approximately eight hundred hadiths. He passed away around 32-33 AH.

Anas bin Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion who served the Prophet Muhammad for ten years in Medina, beginning when he was about ten years old. He was the personal servant of the Prophet and narrated over two thousand hadiths, making him one of the most prolific Companion narrators. He was the last surviving Companion in Basra, where he settled and taught for decades. His narrations cover virtually every aspect of the Prophet's daily life and practice. He passed away around 91-93 AH.

Zayd ibn Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina who participated in seventeen military expeditions. He is renowned for narrating the hadith of Ghadir Khumm in which the Prophet spoke about the Quran and his household. He transmitted many hadiths on theology, prayer, and the Companions' merits, and settled in Kufa. He passed away around 65-68 AH. This is a variant spelling of Zaid bin Arqam.

Anas b. Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was among the most prolific narrators of hadith among the Companions, having served the Prophet Muhammad personally for ten years in Medina. He was entrusted by his mother Umm Sulaym to serve the Prophet when he arrived in Medina. His narrations provide intimate accounts of the Prophet's habits, worship, character, and daily life. He was the last Companion to pass away in Basra and died around 91-93 AH. This entry is a variant form of Anas bin Malik.

Ubayy ibn Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the foremost Companions of the Prophet Muhammad in Quranic knowledge and recitation. He was one of the four Companions whom the Prophet specifically instructed his followers to learn the Quran from. He served as a scribe of the Quranic revelation and was the Imam of the Muslims in Medina in Tarawih prayer during the caliphate of Umar. He narrated hadiths on the Quran, worship, and the Prophet's character. He passed away around 19-30 AH.

Aisha bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her), wife of the Prophet Muhammad, was one of the greatest scholars and narrators in Islamic history. She narrated over two thousand hadiths and was the primary authority on the Prophet's private worship, domestic life, and personal conduct. Companions and Successors frequently consulted her on religious rulings and she issued fatwas on matters affecting women and the broader Muslim community. She passed away in Medina in 58 AH. This entry is an alternate descriptive form of Aisha.

Abu Rafi (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who served as his freed slave and close attendant. He is known for narrating hadiths about the Prophet's personal habits, household conduct, and acts of worship. He is sometimes confused with other narrators sharing the same kunya. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are cited in matters of Islamic law and the Prophet's character. He settled in Medina and narrated traditions to the Successor generation.

Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the ten Companions given the glad tidings of Paradise and among the earliest to embrace Islam, reportedly the third or sixth person to do so. He was a maternal uncle of the Prophet Muhammad and a skilled archer. He led the Muslim army in the decisive Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, completing the conquest of Persia, and became the first to shed blood in the cause of Islam. He narrated many hadiths and passed away around 55-58 AH.

Ibrahim al-Nakha'i was a leading Successor scholar and jurist from Kufa, considered one of the most important authorities in the Kufan legal school that later developed into the Hanafi madhhab. He narrated from his aunt Aisha, from Anas ibn Malik, and from senior Successor scholars. He was the primary teacher of Hammad ibn Abi Sulayman, who in turn taught Imam Abu Hanifa. He was known for his deep legal knowledge and cautious approach to hadith narration. He passed away around 95-96 AH.

Jabir b. Abd Allah al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Khazraj tribe of Medina. He participated in many battles and narrated approximately one thousand five hundred hadiths covering worship, pilgrimage, commerce, and the Prophet's character.
