His detailed account of the Farewell Pilgrimage is among the most comprehensive preserved descriptions of the rites of Hajj. He passed away in Medina around 73-78 AH. This is a variant form of Jabir bin Abdullah.

Ikrimah was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) and the freed slave of Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), from whom he learned the Quran and its interpretation extensively. He became one of the foremost authorities on Quranic exegesis of his generation, traveling widely across the Islamic world and narrating from numerous Companions. He is considered an important link in many chains of Quranic tafsir. Some scholars had reservations about his reliability in certain transmitted reports, but the majority of hadith scholars accepted his narrations, and he is found extensively in the collections of Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Ibn Majah.

Jundub ibn Abdullah al-Bajali (or al-Azdi, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in several battles. He is known for narrating hadiths related to the prohibition of sorcery and the obligation to act upon one's faith. He is also reported to have executed a sorcerer, which became a point of discussion among jurists regarding the legal ruling on magic practitioners. His reports appear in the major hadith collections. He passed away in the early Umayyad period.

Amra bint Abdur-Rahman was a leading female scholar of the Tabi'in generation, known for her exceptional mastery of hadith. She was a student of Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) and transmitted a large body of prophetic traditions, particularly regarding matters of family life, worship, and women's affairs. The great imam Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz considered her one of the foremost authorities on Aisha's narrations. She is among the most trusted female hadith narrators in Islamic scholarship. She is counted among the indispensable scholars of her generation for the preservation of Aisha's prophetic legacy.

name_en: Abu Huraira and Zaid bin Khalid Al-Juhani
bio_short_en: Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with him) was the most prolific narrator of hadith among the Companions, having memorized thousands of traditions through his close companionship with the Prophet. Zaid bin Khalid al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who participated in major campaigns and narrated numerous hadith on worship and legal matters. In certain narrations both Companions transmitted together, their combined testimony lending additional weight to the report. Their dual narration illustrates how Companions often corroborated each other's reports, strengthening the reliability of prophetic traditions.

Jabir bin Samura (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who grew up in his presence and narrated numerous hadith. He was among those who prayed behind the Prophet regularly and transmitted descriptions of the Prophet's appearance, prayer, and conduct. His narrations are preserved across the major hadith collections and he is considered a reliable witness to prophetic practice. He lived in Kufa and remained an important source of prophetic traditions for later scholars. His account of the Prophet's Friday sermon conducted while sitting at times is one of his most often-cited narrations.

Abdullah bin Zaid (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet, best known for his narration describing the adhan (call to prayer). He reported that he saw the wording of the adhan in a dream, which he relayed to the Prophet, who confirmed it as divinely inspired. He participated in the major early battles of Islam and is counted among the Ansar of Medina. His most famous narration on the adhan is transmitted in all the major hadith collections.

Zaid bin Khalid al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who settled in Medina and later in Kufa. He was present at the treaty of Hudaybiyyah and participated in important events of early Islamic history. He narrated many hadith on matters of prayer, purification, charity, and legal rulings, and is particularly known for his narration on the rules regarding found property. His narrations are included in all six major hadith collections, and he is regarded as a trustworthy Companion narrator.

Thabit al-Bunani was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and hadith narrator from Basra. He was a devoted student of Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) and transmitted many traditions from him, making him one of the principal preservers of Anas's hadith. Thabit was known for his intense piety, frequent weeping in prayer, and deep attachment to worship. He narrated a large body of hadith covering prayer, the Prophet's character, and Islamic manners. His reports are found extensively in the Sahih collections and other major works of hadith.

Salim bin Abdullah bin Umar was a leading scholar of the Tabi'in generation and one of the seven famous jurists of Medina. The son of the Companion Abdullah bin Umar (may Allah be pleased with him), he transmitted a vast number of hadith from his father and is regarded as one of the most accurate narrators of Ibn Umar's traditions. He was known for his scrupulous adherence to the Sunnah and his father's practice. His narrations are central to the major hadith collections.

Muadh bin Jabal (may Allah be pleased with him) was among the most learned of the Companions in the knowledge of halal and haram. The Prophet described him as the most knowledgeable of his community in jurisprudence and sent him as a teacher and judge to Yemen. He was a young Ansari Companion who embraced Islam early and accompanied the Prophet in all major campaigns. His narrations on prayer, knowledge, and the obligatory duties of Muslims are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Al-Mughira bin Shu'ba (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet, known for his intelligence and diplomatic acumen. He accepted Islam early and participated in the major campaigns of the Prophet's era. He later served as a governor under the caliphs and narrated numerous hadith on matters of purification, prayer, and the Prophet's conduct. His narrations are found across all the major hadith collections, and he is counted among the most politically astute of the Companions.

Al-Qasim bin Muhammad bin Abi Bakr was a leading Tabi'i scholar and one of the seven great jurists of Medina. He was the grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) and a devoted student of Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her), from whom he transmitted a large number of hadith. He was renowned for his knowledge, piety, and mastery of jurisprudence. His narrations are relied upon in the major hadith collections as a bridge to Aisha's traditions.

Abdullah bin Ka'b was a Tabi'i narrator and scholar who transmitted hadith from the Companions, particularly on matters of daily life and worship. He is mentioned in the chains of transmission of several hadith in the major collections. As a member of the Tabi'in generation, he helped preserve and relay prophetic traditions to subsequent scholars, contributing to the continuity of the Sunnah through reliable transmission. His narrations are cited by later scholars in the context of Islamic legal discussions. Scholars of hadith criticism have generally treated him as acceptable in the chains where he appears.

Alqamah bin Qays al-Nakha'i was a leading Tabi'i jurist and hadith scholar of Kufa. He was among the closest students of Abdullah bin Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) and transmitted a great many traditions from him. Alqamah was known for his exceptional understanding of the Quran and the Sunnah, and Ibrahim al-Nakha'i and other major scholars considered him a primary authority. His narrations are found in the Sahih collections and other major works. His students carried his scholarship forward, making him an enduring influence on Kufan jurisprudence and hadith scholarship.

Sa'id, as referenced in hadith chains, most likely refers to Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, the foremost jurist and hadith scholar of the Tabi'in generation in Medina. He studied under numerous Companions, particularly Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with him) and Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), and was renowned for his unmatched mastery of the Sunnah and Islamic law. He was known for his independence and refusal to compromise his principles even before rulers. His legal opinions were authoritative across the Muslim world, and his narrations appear throughout the six major hadith collections.

Abu al-Minhal was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadith from Companions and senior Tabi'in scholars. He is identified in some sources as Abu al-Minhal Sayyar ibn Salamah, a Basran narrator who transmitted from his father and from other Companions. He is mentioned in the chains of narration in several hadith collections and is regarded as a reliable transmitter. His narrations touch on matters of commerce, prayer, and daily Islamic practice. He is a valuable link in the transmission of hadith from the Companion generation to the scholars who came after them.

Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) was one of the earliest and most revered companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He was known for his deep knowledge of the Quran and his meticulous care in narrating hadith, refusing to narrate anything he was not fully certain about. The Prophet singled him out as a model to be followed in religious matters. He became the leading teacher in Kufa during the caliphate of Umar and left behind a rich legacy of narrations and students who became foundational scholars of Islamic jurisprudence.

Abu Jamra al-Duba'i was a Tabi'i hadith narrator from Basra who transmitted extensively from Abdullah bin Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him). He is counted among the reliable narrators of the Tabi'in generation and his reports from Ibn Abbas appear in the major hadith collections. He was known for his close association with Ibn Abbas, from whom he transmitted both hadith and Quranic exegesis. His narrations cover a wide range of topics including prayer, commerce, and prophetic manners. He is particularly valued for his closeness to Ibn Abbas, whose vast Quranic knowledge he helped transmit to the next generation.

Ubai bin Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the foremost Companions in knowledge of the Quran and hadith. The Prophet described him as the best reciter of the Quran among the Companions. He was a senior Ansari Companion and one of the few scribes who wrote down the Quranic revelation during the Prophet's lifetime. His narrations on the Quran, prayer, and the virtues of Surahs are preserved in the major hadith collections. He is universally respected across all schools of Islamic thought as a towering authority on the Quran and its recitation.

Ayyub al-Sakhtiyani was a leading Tabi'i hadith scholar and jurist of Basra. He was among the foremost scholars of his generation, known for his precise transmission of hadith, his deep piety, and his steadfast adherence to the Sunnah. He narrated from senior Tabi'in including Ibn Sirin and Ata ibn Abi Rabah, as well as from Companions. Imam Ahmad, Yahya ibn Ma'in, and others described him as one of the most reliable narrators of his era. His narrations appear throughout the six major hadith collections.

Al-A'mash, Sulayman ibn Mihran, was one of the most prolific and reliable Tabi'i hadith scholars of Kufa. He narrated from numerous Companions and senior Tabi'in and is among the most frequently cited narrators in the Sahih collections. Despite his wide scholarship, he was known for his simple and ascetic lifestyle. His memory and precision in hadith transmission were praised by the leading scholars of his time, making him an indispensable figure in the chains of hadith narration. He is one of the most cited narrators in the six major hadith collections and his precision set the standard for Kufan hadith scholarship.

Jabir bin Abdullah al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet and one of the most prolific narrators of hadith among the Ansar. He accompanied the Prophet on numerous expeditions and narrated over one thousand five hundred traditions on worship, ethics, and law. He lived to a very old age and continued to teach hadith in Medina, making him a key transmitter for the Tabi'in generation. His narrations are found extensively in all the major hadith collections.

Abdur-Rahman bin Yazid al-Nakha'i was a leading Tabi'i jurist and hadith scholar of Kufa, and the uncle of the great scholar Ibrahim al-Nakha'i. He was among the close students of Abdullah bin Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) and transmitted many traditions from him. He accompanied Ibn Mas'ud on the pilgrimage and memorized his practice in detail. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections, particularly on matters of prayer, pilgrimage, and legal rulings. He was a model of Kufan scholarship in the generation immediately following the Companions.

Usama bin Zayd (may Allah be pleased with him) was the son of Zayd bin Haritha and was deeply beloved by the Prophet, who called him and his father among the most beloved people to him. He narrated hadith directly from the Prophet on matters of faith, ethics, and law. He was appointed by the Prophet to lead a military expedition at a young age, reflecting the Prophet's trust in him. His narrations are preserved in the major collections and he is among the well-known Companion narrators.

Aslam was a freed slave and close companion of Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), from whom he transmitted many hadith and accounts of the caliph's life and rulings. He is a reliable Tabi'i narrator whose reports from Umar are preserved in the major hadith collections. His close proximity to Umar made him an important transmitter of prophetic traditions and the caliph's practices. He is considered trustworthy by the scholars of hadith criticism. His eyewitness proximity to Umar made his narrations about the second caliph's conduct uniquely valuable for later scholars.

Abu Talha al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the leading Ansari Companions of the Prophet and one of the strongest archers among the Muslims. He narrated hadith from the Prophet on matters of worship, charity, and daily conduct. He was a devoted Companion who participated in all the major expeditions and was known for his generosity and courage. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is counted among the noble Companions. The Prophet prayed over him and praised his dedication, making him one of the honored Companions of Medina.

Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) was the second Caliph of Islam and one of the most influential Companions of the Prophet. He embraced Islam and his conversion greatly strengthened the early Muslim community. He narrated numerous hadith from the Prophet on all aspects of Islamic life, and his own judgments and practice became foundational references in Islamic jurisprudence. His narrations are among the most relied upon in all the major hadith collections. He is considered one of the two greatest Companions after Abu Bakr and a central pillar of Islamic history.

Imran bin Husain (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who embraced Islam and became one of his devoted followers. He narrated numerous hadith on prayer, purity, and the Prophet's conduct. He was known for his piety and for reportedly experiencing spiritual visions. He served as a teacher in Basra in his later years and is counted among the reliable Companion narrators whose reports are preserved in the major hadith collections. He remained a revered source of hadith in Basra and his narrations are widely cited across the classical hadith literature.

Ubayy bin Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him) was among the most distinguished Companions in Quranic recitation and hadith narration. The Prophet praised him as the master reciter of the Quran and he was one of the official scribes of the revelation. He was a senior Ansari Companion who transmitted many traditions on the Quran, prayer, and Islamic conduct. His narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections and he is recognized as one of the foremost authorities among the Companions.

Sa'eed bin Jubair was a leading Tabi'i scholar and hadith narrator from Kufa. He studied extensively under Abdullah bin Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him) and is regarded as one of the most knowledgeable and pious scholars of his generation. He was executed by the Umayyad governor al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf for refusing to compromise his principles, becoming a symbol of scholarly integrity. His narrations are found in all the major hadith collections and are highly regarded by scholars. He is remembered as one of the most courageous scholars of the Tabi'in for his refusal to submit to unjust authority.

Abu Ayyub al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a noble Companion of the Prophet who hosted the Prophet in his home upon the Prophet's arrival in Medina, an honor he treasured greatly. He participated in the major battles and campaigns, from Badr onward, and narrated numerous hadith on prayer, worship, and the Prophet's conduct. He continued to join military expeditions even in old age and was martyred near Constantinople, where his grave became a site of visitation. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Hudhaifah ibn al-Yaman (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the closest and most trusted Companions of the Prophet, entrusted with the secrets of the hypocrites. The Prophet confided in him information about trials and tribulations to come, making him the Companion most knowledgeable about future fitna. He narrated numerous important hadith on faith, leadership, and eschatology. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and are particularly cited in discussions of Islamic governance and the end times.

Abu Burdah bin Abi Musa al-Ash'ari was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and son of the Companion Abu Musa al-Ash'ari (may Allah be pleased with him). He narrated extensively from his father and other Companions, and served as a judge in Kufa. He was known for his knowledge, reliability, and sound judgment. His narrations appear in all the major hadith collections and he is considered one of the trustworthy bridges between the Companions and the Tabi'in scholars. He played a vital role in transmitting his father's knowledge of the Prophet to the scholars of the Tabi'in generation.

Abu Qatadah al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet renowned for his bravery in battle. He was among the horsemen of the Prophet and was commended for his valor in multiple expeditions, including Uhud and Hunayn. He narrated numerous hadith on prayer, fasting, hunting, and the Prophet's conduct in worship. His narrations on voluntary fasting, the greeting of the masjid prayer, and manners in daily life are well-known and preserved in the major hadith collections.

Abu Rafi' (may Allah be pleased with him) was a freed slave of the Prophet who served him closely for many years. He was among the early converts to Islam and witnessed many aspects of the Prophet's private life, worship, and household conduct. He narrated traditions about the Prophet's prayer, fasting, and daily habits, making his reports particularly valuable for understanding the Prophet's personal practice. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and he is considered a reliable transmitter among the Companions.

Ammar ibn Yasir (may Allah be pleased with him) was among the earliest converts to Islam and among those who suffered the most severe persecution for their faith. He and his parents were tortured by the Quraysh; his mother Sumayyah was the first martyr in Islam. The Prophet declared that Ammar would be killed by the transgressing party, which was fulfilled at the Battle of Siffin. He narrated hadith on faith, prayer, and Islamic conduct, and his narrations are found in the major hadith collections.

Jabir ibn Samurah (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who lived in close proximity to him and observed his worship and conduct. He narrated many hadith on prayer, the Friday sermon, and descriptions of the Prophet's appearance. He grew up around the Prophet from a young age and his narrations carry the freshness of direct observation. His reports are preserved in the Sahih collections and other major hadith works. He was one of the early Muslims of Kufa whose testimony bridged the Companion era with the growing scholarly community there.

Zayd ibn Thabit (may Allah be pleased with him) was the principal scribe of the Quranic revelation and one of the most learned Companions in matters of the Quran and inheritance law. The Prophet entrusted him with recording the revelations and he later oversaw the compilation of the Quran under Abu Bakr and Uthman (may Allah be pleased with them). He narrated numerous hadith on Quranic recitation, prayer, and Islamic jurisprudence. His narrations are preserved across all the major hadith collections.

Ka'b ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the three Companions who stayed behind from the Expedition of Tabuk, whose repentance and its acceptance by Allah are recorded in the Quran. He was a poet of Islam and narrated many hadith on the Prophet's campaigns, conduct, and rulings. His famous account of his own repentance is one of the most detailed personal narratives in the hadith literature. His narrations are preserved in the major collections. He is also remembered for narrating numerous traditions about the Prophet's battles, manners, and rulings.

This entry refers to an unnamed narrator appearing in a hadith chain as 'a man,' whose specific identity is not recorded in the source. Such anonymous narrators, known in hadith sciences as majhul (unknown), appear in some chains of transmission and are generally treated with caution by hadith scholars. In many cases the identity of an unnamed transmitter can be determined by examining parallel chains from other collections. The legal weight of such a narration depends on the strength of the surrounding chain and corroborating transmissions from identified narrators.

Ata ibn Abi Rabah was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) who lived from approximately 27–114 AH and was the foremost jurist of Makkah in his era. He was an Abyssinian freed slave who became one of the most respected scholars of his time. He narrated from numerous Companions including Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and Aisha (RA). Imam al-Shafi'i said about him: 'Ata was from those whose word was trusted in matters of religion.' He served as the chief Mufti of Makkah and is extensively cited in the Sunan collections on matters of Hajj, prayer, and fiqh.

Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) was the closest Companion of the Prophet and the first Caliph of Islam. He was the first adult male to accept Islam and accompanied the Prophet in the migration to Medina. He narrated hadith from the Prophet on all aspects of faith and practice, and his own rulings and practices became foundational references in Islamic jurisprudence. His narrations are among the most trusted in the major hadith collections. His caliphate set the foundational precedents for Islamic governance that subsequent caliphs and scholars relied upon.

Thabit bin ad-Dahhak (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who was among those who pledged the oath of allegiance at the tree in Hudaybiyyah, one of the most honored gatherings in Islamic history. He narrated hadith from the Prophet on matters of vows, oaths, speech, and ethical conduct. He lived through the early Islamic period and transmitted prophetic traditions to the Tabi'in generation. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections, including Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Abu Humaid as-Sa'idi (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina. He is particularly known for his detailed narration describing the proper way to perform the prayer, including the positions and movements the Prophet used. This hadith is considered one of the most important descriptions of the prophetic prayer and is widely cited by scholars of all legal schools. His other narrations on worship and conduct are also preserved in the major collections.

Ubaidullah bin Abdullah bin Utbah ibn Mas'ud was a leading Tabi'i scholar and one of the seven great jurists of Medina. He was a student of major Companions, including Aisha and Ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with them), and transmitted many traditions from them. He was also a poet and a trusted narrator whose reports appear in the major hadith collections. He is counted among the most reliable scholars bridging the Companions and later Islamic scholarship. His narrations form an important part of the Medinan transmission of prophetic knowledge across multiple fields of Islamic learning.

Al-Miswar bin Makhrama (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who was born in Mecca and later lived in Medina. Marwan bin al-Hakam was an Umayyad official who also narrated hadith. These two figures appear together in certain narrations concerning the treaty of Hudaybiyyah and related events. Al-Miswar's reports from the Prophet and from Abu Bakr and Umar (may Allah be pleased with them) are preserved in the major hadith collections. Their joint narrations concerning the treaty of Hudaybiyyah are among the most detailed accounts of that pivotal event in Islamic history.

Abdullah bin Hisham (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who accompanied him as a child. His mother brought him to the Prophet, who wiped over his head and supplicated for him. He narrated hadith and is counted among the Companions who transmitted prophetic traditions to the next generation. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections, and he is among the younger Companions who witnessed and reported the Prophet's conduct. He is considered a Companion of the Prophet even though he was young during the Prophet's lifetime.

Marwan bin al-Hakam was an Umayyad official and narrator of hadith. Al-Miswar bin Makhrama (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet known for his piety and transmission of hadith. The two appear together in several narrations about the treaty of Hudaybiyyah and related events. Al-Miswar's narrations carry the weight of a Companion's witness, while Marwan's reports are evaluated on the basis of his personal reliability as assessed by hadith critics. Together their combined accounts provide some of the most detailed surviving records of the Hudaybiyyah treaty negotiations.

Al-Qasim, most likely al-Qasim bin Muhammad bin Abi Bakr, was a leading Tabi'i scholar and one of the seven jurists of Medina. He was the grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) and a devoted student of Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her). He transmitted many hadith from her and is regarded as one of the most reliable narrators of Aisha's traditions. His narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections. He is widely regarded as one of the most trustworthy transmitters of prophetic knowledge in the generation following the Companions.

Abdullah bin Buhaina (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet from the tribe of Azd, allied with the Banu Abd al-Muttalib. He narrated hadith from the Prophet on prayer and worship, including the well-known narration about the Prophet performing two prostrations of forgetfulness after rising from the second rak'ah without sitting for tashahhud. This report is a primary evidence for the prostration of forgetfulness in all the major legal schools. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Shaqiq bin Salama al-Asadi, known as Abu Wa'il, was a leading Tabi'i scholar and hadith narrator of Kufa. He was among the close students of Abdullah bin Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) and transmitted many traditions from him and other Companions. He was known for his reliability, piety, and depth of knowledge. His narrations appear extensively in all the major hadith collections and he is regarded as one of the most trustworthy narrators of the Tabi'in generation. He lived to an advanced age and remained a primary source of Ibn Mas'ud's traditions for the scholars of Kufa.

Abu Said al-Khudri (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who narrated a large number of hadiths, particularly on matters of purification, prayer, and eschatology. He was among the companions who devoted themselves to transmitting prophetic knowledge after the Prophet's death and was known for his piety and commitment to accuracy. He actively discouraged writing hadiths in his early years but was nonetheless a major oral transmitter. His narrations are considered highly reliable and found extensively in the major collections.

Ash-Sha'bi, Amir ibn Sharahil al-Sha'bi, was one of the foremost Tabi'i scholars and hadith narrators of Kufa. He claimed to have met five hundred Companions and transmitted from many of them directly. He was renowned for his encyclopedic memory, sharp wit, and mastery of legal rulings and served as a judge and an ambassador. Scholars praised him as an unparalleled authority in both hadith and jurisprudence, and his narrations appear extensively in all the major hadith collections. He is regarded as one of the greatest Tabi'i scholars ever produced by the city of Kufa.

Abbad bin Tamim was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadith from his uncle Abdullah bin Zayd al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him), the Companion known for the narration of the adhan. He relayed traditions about prayer and purification, and his chain through his uncle to the Prophet is found in the major hadith collections. He is regarded as a reliable narrator among the Tabi'in and his reports are preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari and other collections. His chain through his uncle to the Prophet is relied upon as sound by the major hadith scholars.

Amr bin Maimun al-Awdi was a senior Tabi'i scholar and hadith narrator who lived through the early Islamic period and was reportedly present at the assassination of Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him). He narrated from numerous Companions and was regarded as reliable and knowledgeable. He was among the early converts who moved to Kufa and became a transmitter of prophetic traditions to later generations. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections. He is counted among the reliable bridges between the Companion generation and the growing scholarly community of Kufa.

Hisham, as referenced in hadith chains, most often refers to Hisham ibn Urwah, a leading Tabi'i scholar and hadith narrator of Medina. He was the son of Urwah ibn al-Zubayr and transmitted many traditions from his father and from Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her). He was regarded as highly reliable and knowledgeable, and his narrations are found throughout the six major hadith collections. He is one of the most frequently cited transmitters in the chains of Aisha's hadith.

Abdul Aziz, as referenced in hadith chains, most often refers to Abdul Aziz ibn Suhayb al-Bunani, a reliable Tabi'i narrator from Basra who transmitted extensively from Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him). He is counted among the trustworthy narrators of the Tabi'in generation and his reports appear in the major hadith collections. He preserved a significant portion of Anas's traditions on prayer, daily conduct, and descriptions of the Prophet. His role in preserving the traditions of Anas ibn Malik ensured that Basran prophetic knowledge reached the wider Muslim world.

Hafsa bint Umar (may Allah be pleased with her) was a wife of the Prophet and daughter of Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), making her among the Mothers of the Believers. She was known for her piety, fasting, and devotion to prayer. After the Prophet's passing, the first complete written compilation of the Quran was entrusted to her. She narrated numerous hadith on the Prophet's worship, prayer, and household conduct. Her narrations are preserved in the major collections.

Asim, as referenced in hadith chains, most likely refers to Asim ibn Kulayb al-Jarmawi or Asim ibn Umar ibn Qatadah al-Ansari, both reliable Tabi'i narrators who transmitted from senior Companions and Tabi'in scholars. Asim ibn Kulayb transmitted from his father and from Companions in Kufa and is regarded as trustworthy by hadith critics. Their reports on worship, daily conduct, and legal matters appear in the major hadith collections, serving as important links in chains connecting the Companions to later Islamic scholarship.

Samura bin Jundab (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who grew up with the Muslims after his father's death and was raised in Medina. He narrated a significant number of hadith on prayer, the remembrance of Allah, prophetic dreams, and aspects of Islamic law. He later served as a governor in Basra during the Umayyad period. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is counted among the reliable Companion narrators who transmitted to the Basran scholars.

Ash-Shaybani, most likely Abu Ishaq al-Shaybani (Sulayman ibn Abi Sulayman), was a reliable Tabi'i hadith narrator of Kufa who transmitted from senior Tabi'in scholars including al-Sha'bi and others. He is considered trustworthy by the scholars of hadith criticism and his narrations appear in the major collections including Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim. He serves as an important link in the chains of narration connecting the Companions' traditions to later Kufan scholarship, particularly in matters of prayer and conduct. His narrations contribute to the rich corpus of hadith preserved through the Kufan scholarly tradition.

Alqama ibn Qays al-Nakha'i was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) and major jurist of Kufa, dying approximately 62 AH. He was the most prominent student of Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) and transmitted extensively from him, as well as from Umar, Ali, and Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (RA). He was known for his deep knowledge of the Quran and fiqh and became the chief scholar of Kufa after Ibn Mas'ud's death. Ibrahim al-Nakha'i — who was his nephew — transmitted from him extensively, and through this chain, much of the Kufan jurisprudential tradition was preserved and passed to later generations.

Alqamah bin Qays al-Nakha'i was a leading Tabi'i jurist and hadith scholar of Kufa. He was among the foremost students of Abdullah bin Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) and narrated many traditions from him. Alqamah was recognized for his exceptional knowledge of the Quran, his mastery of jurisprudence, and his reliable narration of hadith. He is counted among the most important Tabi'in scholars of Kufa and his narrations appear in the major hadith collections. He is regarded as one of the foremost authorities on the prophetic practice as transmitted through the school of Ibn Mas'ud.

Abu Usaid al-Sa'idi (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina and a participant in the Battle of Badr. He is reported to have been the last surviving Badri Companion in Medina, giving him exceptional authority as a transmitter. He narrated hadith from the Prophet on prayer, the description of the women of Paradise, and various aspects of prophetic conduct. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and are valued for their direct witness to the Prophet.

Zahdam ibn Mudrab al-Jarmi was a Tabi'i narrator from Basra who transmitted hadith from Abu Musa al-Ash'ari (may Allah be pleased with him) and other senior Companions. He is regarded as a reliable narrator by the scholars of hadith criticism, and his reports are mentioned in the chains of several narrations in the major hadith collections. His narrations include accounts of the Prophet's dietary practice and manners, and he served as an important link in preserving the traditions of Abu Musa for later scholars.

Al-Musayyib bin Hazn was a Companion of the Prophet (may Allah be pleased with him) and the father of the great Tabi'i jurist Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib. He narrated a small number of traditions and his name appears in certain chains of hadith. His most famous mention in the sources relates to the account of the death of the Prophet's uncle Abu Talib. His son Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib became one of the greatest scholars of the Tabi'in generation. His importance in Islamic tradition is inseparable from the legacy of his son Sa'id, whose scholarship shaped Islamic jurisprudence.

Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was the personal servant of the Prophet for approximately ten years and one of the most prolific narrators of hadith among the Companions. He served the Prophet from the time of his arrival in Medina until the Prophet's death and witnessed countless aspects of his daily life and worship. He narrated over two thousand traditions and lived to a very old age, making him a primary source for the Tabi'in generation. His narrations are central to all major hadith collections.

Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) was the beloved wife of the Prophet and one of the greatest scholars among the Companions. She narrated over two thousand hadith and was regarded as the foremost authority on matters of the Prophet's private life, prayer, and household conduct. She was also a distinguished jurist whose legal opinions were sought by Companions and Tabi'in alike. Her narrations are among the most important in all the major hadith collections. She continued to teach and guide the Muslim community for decades after the Prophet's passing and is a primary source for half of Islamic knowledge.

Abdullah bin Amr ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet and one of the most prolific narrators of hadith. He had the special permission from the Prophet to write down hadith and maintained a collection known as al-Sahifah al-Sadiqah. He was known for his intense devotion to worship, fasting, and Quran recitation, to the point where the Prophet advised him to take a more moderate approach. His narrations are preserved in all the major hadith collections.

Umm Salamah (may Allah be pleased with her) was a wife of the Prophet and among the Mothers of the Believers. She was known for her wisdom, piety, and knowledge of Islamic rulings, particularly those related to women. She narrated numerous hadith from the Prophet on prayer, dress, mourning, and many other subjects. After the Prophet's passing she continued to be consulted by Companions and Tabi'in scholars on religious matters. Her narrations are preserved in all the major hadith collections.

Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him) was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet, and the fourth Caliph of Islam. He was among the earliest converts to Islam and was deeply knowledgeable in the Quran, Sunnah, and Islamic jurisprudence. He narrated numerous hadith from the Prophet and his own legal rulings are foundational to Islamic scholarship. He was known for his courage, knowledge, and piety. His narrations are preserved throughout the major hadith collections. He is among the most revered of all the Companions and his knowledge of Islam is considered a primary source for the entire community.

Aisha bint Abi Bakr (RA) was the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA). She is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting over 2,210 traditions, and is regarded as a foundational source for knowledge of the Prophet's private conduct and household life. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Take half of your religion from this Humaira (Aisha).' She was renowned for her intelligence, memory, and deep understanding of Islamic jurisprudence. After the Prophet's death, the Companions would refer to her when they needed clarification on religious matters. She died in 57 AH.

Hafsa bint Umar (may Allah be pleased with her), Umm al-Mu'minin, was a wife of the Prophet and a Mother of the Believers. She was the daughter of the Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) and was known for her devotion to fasting and night prayer. The original written compilation of the Quran was kept in her custody after Abu Bakr's caliphate. She narrated numerous hadith on the Prophet's worship and conduct. Her narrations are preserved in the major collections.

Umm Habibah bint Abi Sufyan (may Allah be pleased with her) was a wife of the Prophet and a Mother of the Believers. She was among the early converts who emigrated to Abyssinia to escape persecution in Mecca. She narrated hadith from the Prophet on prayer, worship, and daily conduct. Her father Abu Sufyan later accepted Islam. Her narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and she is counted among the Companions who contributed to the preservation of the Sunnah.

name_en: A'ishah (May Allah be pleased with her)
bio_short_en: Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) was the wife of the Prophet and one of the most accomplished scholars among the Companions. She narrated more than two thousand hadith covering all aspects of Islamic life and was regarded as the foremost authority on the Prophet's private conduct and household affairs. Her knowledge of Islamic jurisprudence, Quran, and medicine was unmatched, and scholars from across the Muslim world traveled to Medina to learn from her. Her narrations are central to all the major hadith collections.

Mu'adh bin Jabal (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the most learned Companions in halal and haram and in the knowledge of Islamic jurisprudence. The Prophet praised him as the most knowledgeable of his community in these matters and sent him as a teacher and judge to Yemen. He was a young Ansari Companion who embraced Islam in its early days and participated in all the major campaigns. His narrations on the pillars of Islam, prayer, and legal matters are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Ibn Abi Laila, Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Laila, was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and jurist of Kufa who served as a judge for many years. He transmitted hadith from numerous Companions, including Ali ibn Abi Talib, Hudhaifah, and others (may Allah be pleased with them). His father Abi Laila was also a Companion of the Prophet, giving Ibn Abi Laila direct family access to prophetic tradition. He is counted among the reliable narrators of the Tabi'in generation and his reports appear in the major hadith collections.

Malik ibn Anas was the founder of the Maliki school of jurisprudence and one of the greatest hadith scholars of all time. He spent his entire scholarly life in Medina, narrating and teaching the Sunnah of the Prophet with meticulous care. His famous work al-Muwatta is one of the earliest and most authoritative compilations of hadith and Islamic law. Imam al-Shafi'i said that after the book of Allah, there is no book on earth more correct than al-Muwatta. His narrations are foundational across all the major collections.

Abu Bakrah (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet who embraced Islam at the conquest of Ta'if. He narrated numerous hadith on Islamic ethics, leadership, and the Day of Judgment. His narration that a people who entrust their affairs to a woman will not succeed is among the most widely discussed hadith in Islamic political thought. He settled in Basra and became one of its principal hadith transmitters. His narrations are preserved in the major collections.

Adi ibn Hatim al-Ta'i (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet and son of the famous pre-Islamic Arab chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i, renowned for his generosity. Adi initially fled to Syria upon hearing of the Prophet's mission but later returned and embraced Islam, becoming a devoted Companion. He narrated hadith from the Prophet on hunting, prayer, and Islamic conduct. He participated in the early Islamic conquests and his narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Abu Ubaid al-Qasim ibn Sallam was a distinguished Islamic scholar of the third century of Islam, known for his encyclopedic knowledge of hadith, Quranic sciences, Arabic language, and Islamic law. He authored several important works and is regarded as a highly reliable narrator and a major authority in Islamic scholarship. His works on Quranic recitation and hadith are still studied by scholars today. He is considered one of the great scholars who contributed to the systematic preservation of Islamic sciences.

Sa'id ibn Zayd (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the ten Companions given glad tidings of Paradise by the Prophet during his lifetime, the group known as al-Ashara al-Mubashshara. He was the son of Zayd ibn Amr, who had turned away from idolatry before Islam, and the husband of Fatimah bint al-Khattab, the sister of Umar. He embraced Islam early and participated in many campaigns. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections. He is among the noblest Companions, and his witnessing of the Prophet's promise of Paradise to him is one of the most honored distinctions in Islamic tradition.

Umm Atiyyah al-Ansariyyah, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in several military expeditions, tending to the wounded and ill. She was known for her close relationship with the Prophet's household and narrated numerous hadiths concerning women's religious practices, including rulings on washing the deceased, funeral rites, and restrictions on mourning. Her narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and remain a primary reference for Islamic jurisprudence related to women.

Hakim ibn Hizam, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and a nephew of Khadijah, the Prophet's first wife. Born inside the Kaaba, he embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca and lived to nearly one hundred years of age. He was renowned for his generosity, his extensive business dealings conducted with scrupulous honesty, and his narrations on commercial ethics that are widely cited in books of fiqh.

Amr ibn Shu'aib was a prominent Tabi'i scholar from Medina and the grandson of the companion Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As, may Allah be pleased with them. He transmitted many hadiths through the chain known as Amr ibn Shu'aib, from his father, from his grandfather — a well-known and debated isnad among hadith scholars. He was a reliable narrator whose reports on legal matters, particularly in commerce and family law, are frequently cited in the four major schools of jurisprudence.

Amir ibn Sharahil al-Sha'bi was one of the greatest Tabi'i scholars of the early Islamic period, known for his encyclopedic knowledge and sharp wit. Born around 19 AH in Kufa, he met many companions of the Prophet and transmitted from them directly, making him a prolific and highly reliable link in many hadith chains. He was a leading jurist of his era, respected by scholars for his broad learning in fiqh, history, and the Quran.

Muhammad ibn al-Munkadir was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar and one of the most trusted hadith transmitters in Medina. A son of the companion al-Munkadir, he narrated from numerous companions including Jabir ibn Abdullah, Aisha, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them all. He was celebrated for his piety, asceticism, and the authenticity of his narrations, which appear in all the major hadith collections.

Zainab bint Abi Salama, may Allah be pleased with her, was a stepdaughter of the Prophet Muhammad through his marriage to Umm Salama. Raised in the Prophet's household, she had direct access to his teachings and the practices of his wives. She narrated hadiths on matters of household conduct, mourning, and women's worship, and her narrations are regarded as highly authentic given her proximity to the Prophet's family.

Isma'il ibn Abi Khalid was a reliable Tabi'i hadith narrator from Kufa who transmitted from a number of senior companions and their followers. He was praised by leading hadith critics for his precision and trustworthiness, and his reports appear regularly in the Sahihayn and other major collections. He was particularly known for his transmission of narrations related to Islamic practice and belief from the scholars of Kufa.

Abu al-Bakhtari, whose name was Sa'id ibn Fayruz al-Ta'i, was a trustworthy Tabi'i scholar from Kufa. He narrated from companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, Ibn Abbas, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them. Known for his learning and piety, his narrations appear in the major hadith collections, and he was respected by scholars of hadith criticism for the reliability of his transmission.

This dual attribution refers to a hadith reported jointly on the authority of both Aisha (RA) and Ibn Abbas (RA), two of the most prolific and scholarly Companions. Aisha (RA) narrated over 2,200 hadiths as the Prophet's wife and a leading jurist, while Ibn Abbas (RA), the Prophet's cousin, narrated over 1,600 hadiths and is known as Tarjuman al-Quran. When a hadith is attributed to both narrators, it indicates parallel transmission of the same prophetic statement through two independent chains, which strengthens the authenticity and transmission of the report.

Abu Tha'laba al-Khushani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the tribe of Khushain. He pledged allegiance to the Prophet and narrated hadiths on various religious rulings including hunting, eating, and general conduct. His narrations are found in several of the six major hadith collections, and he is considered a reliable witness to the Prophet's teachings and practice.

Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a distinguished companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He had the great honor of hosting the Prophet in his home upon the Prophet's arrival in Medina during the Hijra. He participated in many military campaigns and continued striving in the path of Allah into old age, dying during the siege of Constantinople, where he was buried. His narrations on prayer, worship, and general practice are widely cited.

Imran ibn Husayn, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam in the seventh year after the Hijra. He was known for his asceticism, his notable spiritual states, and the angels' greetings reported in his narrations. He settled in Basra, where he became a leading religious authority, and his hadiths on acts of worship, legal rulings, and morality appear in all the major hadith collections.

Shaqiq ibn Salama al-Asadi, also known as Abu Wa'il, was a prominent Tabi'i scholar from Kufa who narrated from many companions of the Prophet including Abdullah ibn Masud, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, and Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with them. He was held in the highest regard by hadith scholars for his reliability and long chain of transmission. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and throughout the major hadith literature.

Mus'ab ibn Sa'd was the son of the illustrious companion Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise. Mus'ab narrated from his father and other companions, transmitting important hadiths on worship, prayer, and the virtues of the companions. He was a trustworthy Tabi'i narrator whose reports appear in the major hadith collections.

Warrad was the secretary and freed slave of Al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah, the companion of the Prophet, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths on behalf of his master, transmitting reports including the famous supplication recited after the prayer. Hadith scholars regarded him as a reliable transmitter, and his narrations, though limited, appear in the Sahihayn and other major collections.

Muhammad ibn Sirin was one of the foremost Tabi'i scholars of Basra, celebrated equally for his mastery of hadith, his expertise in dream interpretation, and his exceptional piety. He narrated from prominent companions including Abu Hurairah, Anas ibn Malik, and Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them. His strict standards of hadith transmission — he was among the first to insist on scrutinizing the chain of narrators — made him a towering figure in the science of hadith criticism.

Jundab ibn Abdullah al-Bajali, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad known for his straightforward and austere character. He narrated hadiths on faith, repentance, and sincere worship, including well-known reports warning against ostentation. He later settled in Basra and played a role in early Islamic governance. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Amr ibn al-Harith, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and a brother-in-law through the Prophet's wife Juwayriyah. He was known for his modest lifestyle and narrated that the Prophet left no worldly wealth at the time of his death save a white mule, weapons, and a small piece of land dedicated as charity. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn.

Ata ibn Abi Rabah was a leading Successor (Tabi'i) who lived from approximately 27–114 AH and was the foremost jurist of Makkah in his era. He was an Abyssinian freed slave who became one of the most respected scholars of his time. He narrated from numerous Companions including Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and Aisha (RA). Imam al-Shafi'i said about him: 'Ata was from those whose word was trusted in matters of religion.' He served as the chief Mufti of Makkah and is extensively cited in the Sunan collections on matters of Hajj, prayer, and fiqh.

Muhammad ibn Jubayr ibn Mut'im was a Tabi'i scholar and son of the companion Jubayr ibn Mut'im, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths from his father and other companions, transmitting reports on topics including the names of the Prophet, matters of lineage, and aspects of worship. He was considered a reliable narrator whose reports appear in the major hadith collections.

Abu al-Darda, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad renowned for his deep wisdom, asceticism, and love of knowledge. He embraced Islam at the time of the Battle of Badr and became one of the foremost scholars among the companions. He settled in Syria and served as a judge and teacher there, narrating many hadiths on worship, the Quran, and spiritual guidance.

Abdur-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first caliph. He embraced Islam and narrated hadiths from the Prophet Muhammad, including narrations related to the People of the Suffa and charitable giving. He was known for his bravery in early Islamic battles and his close connection to the Prophet's household through his family.

Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the ten companions promised Paradise and among the earliest to embrace Islam. A successful merchant, he was known for his extraordinary generosity, once donating half his wealth in charity. He narrated hadiths on prayer, fasting, trade, and governance, and his narrations are preserved across all the major hadith collections.

Suwaid ibn al-Nu'man, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in the expedition to Khaybar and narrated hadiths about the Prophet's practices during that journey, including how the Prophet handled food provisions. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and are cited in discussions of Islamic dietary and travel law.

Abdur-Rahman ibn Abi Layla was a Successor (Tabi'i) and jurist of Kufa who died approximately 83 AH. His father, Abi Layla, was a Companion of the Prophet ﷺ. Abdur-Rahman narrated from numerous Companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, Muadh ibn Jabal, Umar ibn al-Khattab, and others. He became a major scholarly figure in Kufa and was appointed as a judge (qadi) there. He is cited in various chapters of the Sunan collections and is considered a reliable narrator. He was present at several major battles during the period of the early caliphate.

Abdullah ibn Masud, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most eminent companions of the Prophet Muhammad and the first to recite the Quran openly in Mecca. He was the Prophet's personal servant for a time and was deeply trusted for his understanding of Quranic recitation and Islamic law. He later settled in Kufa as a teacher and religious authority, and his vast corpus of narrations covers every domain of Islamic practice.

Khalid ibn al-Walid, may Allah be pleased with him, known as the Sword of Allah, was one of the greatest military commanders in Islamic history. He embraced Islam in the eighth year after the Hijra and thereafter led Muslims to remarkable victories in Arabia, Syria, Iraq, and Persia. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and his narrations on permissible foods, moral conduct, and other matters appear in the major hadith collections.

Tariq ibn Shihab al-Ahmasi was a Tabi'i narrator from Kufa who saw the Prophet Muhammad as a child though did not narrate from him directly. He narrated extensively from senior companions including Abu Bakr, Umar, Ali, and Hudhayfah, may Allah be pleased with them, and was regarded as a reliable transmitter by hadith scholars. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and other major collections.

Umm Habibah, may Allah be pleased with her, whose name was Ramlah bint Abi Sufyan, was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad and daughter of Abu Sufyan. She emigrated to Abyssinia in the early years of Islam and later married the Prophet, who sent for her from Abyssinia. She narrated hadiths on prayer, worship, and the Prophet's conduct, and her narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Abdullah ibn Masud, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most learned and trusted companions of the Prophet Muhammad. He memorized more than seventy surahs directly from the Prophet's lips and was one of four companions the Prophet commanded his followers to learn the Quran from. His juristic opinions formed the basis of the Hanafi school, and he narrated hundreds of hadiths on all aspects of Islamic practice and belief.

Abu Salamah ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was a leading Tabi'i scholar from Medina and the son of the companion Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, may Allah be pleased with him. He was a frequent transmitter of hadiths from Aisha, Abu Hurairah, and other companions, and his narrations appear throughout the Sahihayn and the Sunan collections. He was regarded as one of the seven leading jurists of Medina.

Abdullah ibn Buraidah was a Tabi'i scholar from Khurasan and the son of the companion Buraidah ibn al-Husayb, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths from his father and other companions, transmitting reports on prayer, fasting, military campaigns, and supplications. He served as a judge in Marw and was considered a reliable narrator by the scholars of hadith.

Bahz ibn Hakim was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths through the well-known chain from his grandfather Mu'awiyah ibn Haydah, a companion of the Prophet, may Allah be pleased with him. His narrations, while sometimes debated among hadith critics, cover topics including the rights of neighbors, modesty, and charitable giving. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are cited in books of jurisprudence.

Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab and one of the most prolific narrators among the companions. Devoted to following the Prophet's example in every detail, he was known for his meticulous piety and his scrupulous adherence to the Sunnah. He narrated approximately 2,630 hadiths covering all aspects of Islamic life, and his narrations are foundational to Islamic jurisprudence.

Amr ibn al-As, may Allah be pleased with him, was a brilliant military strategist and companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam in the eighth year after the Hijra. He led the Muslim conquest of Egypt and served as its governor. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on topics including sincere repentance, prayer, and governance, and his narrations are found in the major hadith collections.

Abdullah ibn Mughaffal, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of those who gave the pledge of allegiance under the tree at Hudaybiyyah. He narrated hadiths on prayer, the Quran, and the Prophet's habits and manner of recitation. He settled in Basra after the conquests and became a local religious authority there.

Zayd ibn Khalid al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the expedition of Hudaybiyyah. He narrated hadiths on prayer, fasting, financial transactions, and legal rulings including on lost property and testimony. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and the four Sunan collections.

Wathilah ibn al-Asqa, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a number of hadiths on faith, sincerity, and righteous conduct. He lived to a very advanced age, reportedly dying at over one hundred years old in Syria, where he became a known narrator in the later generation of companions. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections.

Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who served as one of his scribes recording the revelation. He later became the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate and served as caliph for nearly twenty years, during which the Islamic state expanded greatly. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on worship, governance, and general conduct.

Abdullah ibn Busr, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who was among the youngest to narrate from him directly. He lived to a great age and was the last companion to die in Syria, allowing later generations to benefit from his direct link to the Prophet. He narrated hadiths on supplication, prayer, fasting, and the merit of various acts of worship.

Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad and is regarded as the greatest Quranic exegete among the companions. The Prophet supplicated for him specifically, asking Allah to grant him understanding of the religion, and he fulfilled that prayer through his unparalleled scholarship. He narrated thousands of hadiths and his explanations of the Quran form the foundation of the science of tafsir.

Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was a devoted companion of the Prophet who embraced Islam as a young man alongside his father Umar ibn al-Khattab. He was deeply committed to emulating the Prophet's Sunnah in every detail and narrated extensively from both the Prophet and from Abu Bakr, his father Umar, and other senior companions. His narrations are among the most frequently cited in Islamic jurisprudence.

Ya'la ibn Umayyah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in various military campaigns. He narrated hadiths on the rites of Hajj, dress for pilgrims in the state of ihram, and related matters. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and other major collections and are cited in discussions of Hajj jurisprudence.

Kuraib was a freed slave of Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, and a reliable Tabi'i transmitter who narrated extensively on behalf of his master. He served as a secretary to Ibn Abbas and transmitted many important hadiths from him, including those related to prayer, the beginning of months, and legal rulings. Hadith scholars regarded him as trustworthy and his narrations appear in the major collections.

Umm Hani bint Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with her, was the daughter of Abu Talib and thus a cousin of the Prophet Muhammad. She is associated with one of the most significant events in Islamic history — the Night Journey and Ascension — as the Prophet is reported to have departed from her home that night. She narrated hadiths on prayer, the Night Journey, and other matters, and her narrations appear in the major hadith collections.

Abu Tha'labah al-Khushani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad known for his narrations on permissible and impermissible food, particularly the ruling on hunting with trained animals and dogs. He pledged allegiance to the Prophet and narrated hadiths on various legal and ethical matters that are preserved in the Sahihayn and the Sunan works.

Rafi ibn Khadij, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in numerous military campaigns and was wounded at the Battle of Uhud. He narrated important hadiths on agricultural contracts, particularly sharecropping arrangements, which shaped Islamic commercial law. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and the Sunan collections.

Usamah ibn Zayd, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Zayd ibn Harithah, the freed slave and beloved companion of the Prophet, and was himself deeply loved by the Prophet, who called him and his father among his most beloved people. He was appointed commander of a military expedition at a young age. He narrated hadiths on worship, the Prophet's character, and legal rulings, and his narrations appear in the major collections.

Al-Miqdam ibn Ma'dikarib, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Syria. He narrated hadiths on the authority of the Sunnah alongside the Quran, the rights of guests, the prohibition of killing certain animals, and other matters. He lived to an advanced age in Syria and narrated many hadiths that appear in the Sunan works and other major collections.

Al-Nu'man ibn Bashir, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and the first Ansari child born after the Hijra. He narrated the well-known hadith comparing lawful and unlawful matters to clear and doubtful gray areas, which is considered one of the foundational principles of Islamic jurisprudence. He served as governor of Kufa and Homs and narrated many hadiths preserved in the Sahihayn.

Mu'adh ibn Anas al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated hadiths on the merit of patience, supplication, and various acts of worship. He settled in Egypt after the conquests and became a recognized religious authority there. His narrations appear in the Sunan works and other major hadith collections.

AbuRimthah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a description of the Prophet's physical appearance, including details of the Prophet's hair color and build. His account is among those cited in works on the Prophet's physical attributes (shama'il). His narrations appear in the Sunan of al-Tirmidhi and other collections.

This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Abu Hurairah and Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, may Allah be pleased with them both. Abu Hurairah was the most prolific narrator of hadith among all companions, while Abu Sa'id al-Khudri was a major narrator on matters of worship, social ethics, and eschatology. Their combined narrations form a vast corpus covering virtually every dimension of Islamic practice.

This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Aisha, may Allah be pleased with her, and Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him. Aisha was the most knowledgeable of the Prophet's wives and the most prolific female narrator of hadith, while Ibn Abbas was the foremost exegete of the Quran among the companions. Together they represent two of Islam's greatest authorities on religious knowledge.

Al-Ahnaf ibn Qais al-Tamimi was a leading Tabi'i figure renowned for his wisdom, forbearance, and eloquence. He lived during the time of the companions and narrated from several of them including Umar, Ali, and Abu Bakr, may Allah be pleased with them. He was regarded as one of the wisest Arabs of his generation and played an important role in early Islamic governance in Basra.

name_en: Zaid bin Khalid and Abu Huraira
bio_short_en: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Zayd ibn Khalid al-Juhani and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them both. Zayd was an Ansari companion who narrated on legal matters including lost property and testimony, while Abu Hurairah was the most prolific narrator of hadith overall. Their combined narrations cover a wide range of legal and ethical topics.

Shu'bah ibn al-Hajjaj was one of the most eminent hadith scholars of Basra and a pioneer in the systematic criticism of narrators. He was the first scholar to apply rigorous scrutiny to the reliability of transmitters, earning the title Prince of the Believers in Hadith from some scholars. He narrated from hundreds of Tabi'in and his high standards of authentication made his seal of approval a benchmark of reliability in hadith scholarship.

name_en: Marwan bin Al-Hakam and Al-Miswar bin Makhrama
bio_short_en: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly by Marwan ibn al-Hakam and al-Miswar ibn Makhrama, may Allah be pleased with al-Miswar. Al-Miswar was a companion of the Prophet who narrated important hadiths on the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and related events. Marwan transmitted alongside him in several key narrations concerning the Prophet's era. Their combined accounts are valuable historical records of events from the Prophet's time.

Mujashi ibn Masud al-Sulami, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the pledges of allegiance to the Prophet. He narrated hadiths on the conditions and significance of the pledge of allegiance (bay'ah) and other matters of Islamic practice. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn.

Salim Abu al-Nadr was a reliable Tabi'i narrator and freed slave of Umar ibn Ubaydullah al-Taymi in Medina. He narrated hadiths from Companions and senior Tabi'in, including the famous narration of the letter of Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa regarding the ruling on fighting during the months of truce. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and are cited by hadith scholars as trustworthy.

Abu Uthman al-Nahdi, whose name was Abd al-Rahman ibn Mall, was a senior Tabi'i scholar who narrated from dozens of companions including Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali, may Allah be pleased with them. He lived to a very advanced age and was one of the most prolific Tabi'i narrators of his era. His narrations appear extensively in the Sahihayn and the Sunan works.

This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Aisha, may Allah be pleased with her, the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad, and Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with them, the Prophet's cousin and great Quranic scholar. Aisha was the most prolific female narrator of hadith, while Ibn Abbas was renowned for his depth of Quranic interpretation and legal insight. Together their combined corpus covers virtually every domain of Islamic knowledge.

This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by al-Bara ibn Azib and Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa, may Allah be pleased with them both. Al-Bara was a young Ansari companion who participated in fifteen expeditions and narrated widely on the Prophet's practice. Ibn Abi Awfa was among the last companions to die in Kufa. Together their narrations provide important accounts of the Prophet's military campaigns and general religious practice.

Ubaidullah ibn Abdullah ibn Utbah ibn Masud was a leading Tabi'i scholar of Medina and one of the seven great jurists of Medina. He narrated from prominent companions including Aisha, Ibn Abbas, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them, and was a teacher of the renowned Imam al-Zuhri. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and throughout the major hadith collections.

Abdullah ibn Qais, known as Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a prominent companion of the Prophet Muhammad and a leading scholar of the Quran, hadith, and Islamic law. He served as governor of Basra and Kufa and was renowned for the beauty of his Quranic recitation. He narrated extensively on matters of prayer, fasting, and legal rulings, and his narrations are found across all the major hadith collections.

Talha ibn Ubaydillah, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise and among the earliest to embrace Islam. He was celebrated for his extraordinary bravery at the Battle of Uhud, where he shielded the Prophet from arrows with his own body, earning him the title Talha the Generous and Talha the Good. He narrated hadiths on worship and the Prophet's character preserved in the major collections.

Asma bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her, was the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and elder sister of Aisha, earning the epithet Dhat al-Nitaqayn (She of the Two Waistbands) for her role in provisioning the Prophet and her father during the Hijra. She lived to nearly one hundred years of age and narrated numerous hadiths on worship, the Prophet's practice, and historical events from the early Islamic period.

Samura ibn Jundub, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam at a young age. He narrated hadiths on prayer, dreams, and the Prophet's commands regarding various matters of religious practice. He later served as governor of Basra on several occasions and was known as a narrator of the Prophet's traditions across the major hadith collections.

Muhammad ibn al-Munkadir was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar and one of the most trusted hadith transmitters in Medina. He narrated from prominent companions including Jabir ibn Abdullah, Aisha, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them, and was celebrated for his piety, weeping out of fear of Allah, and the authenticity of his narrations. His reports appear in the Sahihayn and all the major hadith collections.

Umar ibn Abi Salama, may Allah be pleased with him, was a stepson of the Prophet Muhammad through his mother Umm Salama. He grew up in the Prophet's household and narrated hadiths on table manners, the correct way of eating, and the Prophet's daily conduct. His narrations provide intimate glimpses into the Prophet's household life and are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Sa'id ibn al-Harith al-Ansari was a Tabi'i narrator from Medina who transmitted from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and other companions of the Prophet, may Allah be pleased with them. He narrated hadiths on prayer including the ruling on reciting the opening chapter al-Fatiha in every unit of prayer. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and are cited in works of jurisprudence.

Itban ibn Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He had difficulty walking to the mosque due to his condition and the Prophet visited his home and prayed there, establishing a personal prayer place for him. He narrated the famous hadith affirming that sincere profession of faith protects a person from Hellfire, a narration preserved in the Sahihayn.

Ka'b ibn Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the three companions who remained behind from the expedition of Tabuk. His account of the story of those who stayed behind — and the sincere repentance that followed — is one of the most moving narratives in the hadith literature, preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari. He was also a renowned poet who used his talent in defense of Islam.

Awn ibn Abi Juhayfah was a Tabi'i narrator and the son of the companion Abu Juhayfah, may Allah be pleased with him. He transmitted hadiths from his father and other companions, preserving important narrations including descriptions of the Prophet's physical appearance and his practices in prayer. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and are cited by scholars in discussions of the Prophet's conduct and worship.

Malik ibn al-Huwairith, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who came to Medina as a young man with a group from his tribe to learn Islam. The Prophet gave him and his companions direct instruction in the performance of prayer, telling them to pray as they had seen him pray. He narrated hadiths on prayer and the Prophet's practice that are preserved in the Sahihayn.

Anas ibn Sirin was a Tabi'i scholar and the brother of the famous Muhammad ibn Sirin. He narrated from companions including Anas ibn Malik and Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them, and was regarded as a reliable transmitter by hadith scholars. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections, though he is less prolific than his brother.

Abu Ma'mar, whose name was Abdullah ibn Sakhbarah, was a Tabi'i scholar from Kufa who narrated from companions including Abdullah ibn Masud and others. He was regarded as a reliable narrator by hadith scholars and transmitted narrations on prayer and recitation. His reports appear in the major hadith collections and are cited in discussions of Quranic recitation and Islamic practice.

Marwan ibn al-Hakam was a Umayyad ruler who served as caliph and narrated hadiths he received from companions of the Prophet, particularly from Zayd ibn Thabit and Uthman ibn Affan, may Allah be pleased with them. While his political career was controversial among early Muslims, his narrations in the hadith collections are evaluated on their individual merits by hadith scholars.

Abu Wail, whose name was Shaqiq ibn Salama, was a prominent Tabi'i scholar of Kufa and a dedicated student of major companions including Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) and Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA). He was known for his reliability, piety, and commitment to transmitting prophetic knowledge accurately. He narrated widely from the companions and is cited frequently in the major hadith collections as a trusted and authoritative transmitter. His long life allowed him to serve as an important link between the companions and later scholars.

Ata ibn Abi Rabah was one of the foremost Tabi'i scholars of Mecca and a leading authority in hadith and jurisprudence. He was a freed slave of Qurayshi origin who became the chief mufti of Mecca in his time. He studied under numerous Companions, including Abdullah bin Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him), and was recognized as among the most knowledgeable of the Tabi'in in Quranic exegesis and Islamic law. His narrations appear extensively in all the major hadith collections.

Jundab bin Abdullah al-Bajali, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who narrated hadiths and was known for his piety and strict adherence to the Sunnah. He settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death and was among those who transmitted knowledge to the next generation of Muslims. He is particularly known for narrating hadiths related to sincerity of intention and avoiding ostentation in worship.

Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), cousin of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), was known as the 'Scholar of the Ummah' (Hibr al-Ummah). The Prophet supplicated for him to be granted deep understanding of religion.
