Though young at the Prophet's death, he dedicated his life to learning from senior companions and became the foremost authority on Quranic tafsir and Islamic knowledge of his generation. He narrated hundreds of hadiths and his explanations of the Quran remain central to classical scholarship.

Amir bin Sa'd bin Abi Waqqas was a prominent Tabi'i (successor to the companions) and the son of the illustrious companion Sa'd bin Abi Waqqas, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the ten promised Paradise. Amir narrated extensively from his father and other companions, transmitting a significant body of hadith literature to subsequent generations. He was known as a reliable and trustworthy narrator in the chains of transmission.

Abu Al-Aswad al-Du'ali was a distinguished early scholar of Arabic grammar and a companion or senior Tabi'i who narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him. He is credited as one of the founders of Arabic grammatical science and contributed significantly to the preservation of the Arabic language. As a hadith narrator, he transmitted important reports from the companions and was considered trustworthy by scholars of hadith criticism.

Umm al-Fadl Lubabah bint al-Harith, may Allah be pleased with her, was the wife of al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib and thus the Prophet's aunt by marriage. She was among the earliest women to embrace Islam and is said to have been the second woman to accept the faith after Khadijah. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet and was held in great esteem by the companions for her piety and her close relationship to the Prophet's household.

Amra bint Abd al-Rahman, may Allah have mercy on her, was a leading female scholar of the Tabi'in generation and one of the most important transmitters of hadiths from A'ishah, may Allah be pleased with her. She was regarded by scholars such as Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz as among the most reliable authorities on A'ishah's narrations. Her precision in transmitting knowledge made her a pivotal figure in the preservation of hadith, particularly those concerning women's affairs.

Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the second caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab and one of the most prolific narrators among the companions, transmitting over two thousand hadiths. He was renowned for his meticulous adherence to the Prophet's Sunnah, following it in the finest detail. He lived a long life after the Prophet's death and was a major source of religious guidance for the Muslim community throughout the early Islamic period.

Amr bin Ash-Sharid was a companion or early Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from the Prophet or senior companions. He is recorded in hadith chains relating to various aspects of Islamic practice and was known among scholars of hadith sciences as a narrator whose reports are found in the major collections. Details of his biography are preserved primarily through the hadith chains in which his name appears.

Sulaiman bin Surad al-Khuza'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who lived to an advanced age and participated in early Islamic history. He is known for his association with the people of Kufa and is notable in Islamic history for his role in seeking to avenge the death of Husayn ibn Ali. As a hadith narrator, he transmitted reports from the Prophet that are preserved in the major collections.

Zainab bint Jahsh, may Allah be pleased with her, was a wife of the Prophet and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was the daughter of the Prophet's paternal aunt and was known for her generosity, charity, and skill in handicrafts, using the proceeds of her work to help the poor. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet, including reports related to signs of the Hour and matters of worship. She passed away during the caliphate of Umar ibn al-Khattab.

Humaid bin Abdur-Rahman was a trustworthy Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from companions including Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him. He was a jurist from Kufa or Medina whose narrations appear in the major hadith collections. Scholars of hadith sciences classified him as reliable, and his transmissions cover various aspects of Islamic jurisprudence and practice.

Isma'il bin Abi Khalid was a highly regarded Tabi'i narrator from Kufa who transmitted hadiths from numerous companions and senior Tabi'in. He was considered one of the most reliable and accurate narrators of his generation, and scholars of hadith praised him extensively for his precision and memory. His narrations appear in all the major hadith collections, covering a wide range of topics in Islamic practice.

Qais bin Ubad was an early Tabi'i scholar who narrated from companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib and Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, may Allah be pleased with them. He was based in Basra and was known as a reliable transmitter of hadith whose reports are found in the major collections. His narrations contribute importantly to our knowledge of the companions' practice and the early Muslim community.

Umm Khalid bint Khalid ibn Sa'id, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet who emigrated to Abyssinia as a child with her father. She had the honor of being dressed by the Prophet himself in a garment brought from Abyssinia, upon which he praised its quality. She lived a long life and narrated hadiths from the Prophet, and her account of being dressed by him is among the well-known narrations in the collections.

Abdullah ibn az-Zubayr, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the companion Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and the first child born to the Muhajirun in Medina. He narrated from the Prophet and from numerous companions, and was himself a distinguished companion who fought in several major early battles. He later became the caliph of the Muslim world, ruling from Mecca, and was known for his courage, piety, and devotion to worship.

Ka'b bin Ujra, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who narrated an important hadith regarding the shaving of the head during ihram when afflicted with illness, including the well-known verses about the fidyah (expiation). He is also associated with the narration of the complete form of the Abrahamic prayer (salawat) recited in the tashahhud. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Adi ibn Hatim al-Ta'i (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam after initially being a Christian. He was the son of Hatim al-Ta'i, famous across Arabia for his generosity. Adi narrated numerous hadiths and lived a long life, continuing to transmit prophetic knowledge to later generations of scholars. He is known for asking the Prophet detailed questions about Islamic practice, and the resulting narrations provide valuable guidance. His reports are found in all the major hadith collections.

Amr bin Al-As, may Allah be pleased with him, was a brilliant military commander and statesman who embraced Islam before the conquest of Mecca and became one of the Prophet's most capable companions. He is celebrated for leading the Islamic conquest of Egypt and serving as its governor. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet covering various aspects of faith and practice, and his narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Haritha bin Wahb al-Khuza'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and the maternal brother of Ubaydullah ibn Umar ibn al-Khattab. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet, including well-known reports about the people of Paradise and Hell and the qualities that lead to each. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and provide valuable insight into the Prophet's teachings on character and the afterlife.

Abdur-Rahman bin Samura, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who embraced Islam and participated in the Islamic conquests, serving as a commander in Khorasan. The Prophet gave him important advice regarding leadership and oaths, including the well-known hadith cautioning against seeking positions of authority. His narrations are preserved in the major collections and reflect key aspects of the Prophet's guidance.

Anas bin Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, was the personal servant of the Prophet for ten years, from the time the Prophet arrived in Medina until his death. He is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting thousands of reports about the Prophet's words, actions, and character. The Prophet prayed for his long life, abundant wealth, and offspring, and Anas lived to approximately one hundred years, becoming a major source of knowledge for the early Muslim community.

Rafi bin Khadij, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in many of the Prophet's military campaigns and narrated numerous hadiths covering agricultural practices, business transactions, and acts of worship. He is particularly known for narrating the hadith prohibiting certain forms of land rental arrangements. His reports are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Ali bin Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him, was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet, having married his daughter Fatimah. He was among the first to accept Islam and was raised in the Prophet's household. He served as the fourth Rightly Guided Caliph and is one of the most important narrators of hadith, transmitting hundreds of the Prophet's statements and practices. He was renowned for his knowledge, courage, and piety.

name_en: 'Amr bin Shu'aib on his father's authority from his grandfather (RA)
bio_short_en: This chain refers to Amr bin Shu'aib narrating on the authority of his father Shu'aib, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah bin Amr bin al-As, may Allah be pleased with him. Abdullah bin Amr was a prolific companion who was permitted by the Prophet to write down hadiths, and he compiled an early collection called al-Sahifah al-Sadiqah. This three-link chain (Amr from his father from his grandfather) is a well-recognized isnad in hadith literature, with scholars debating its precise degree of reliability.

name_en: 'Amr bin Shu'aib on his father's authority from his grandfather
bio_short_en: This entry refers to the transmission chain of Amr bin Shu'aib, narrating from his father Shu'aib ibn Muhammad, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As, may Allah be pleased with him. Abdullah ibn Amr was among the most dedicated companions in recording the Prophet's sayings with his written notes. The chain, though sometimes debated by hadith specialists regarding whether Shu'aib had direct contact with his grandfather, is accepted by the majority of scholars and appears frequently in the Sunan collections.

Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him, was the most prolific narrator of hadith among all the companions, transmitting over five thousand reports from the Prophet. He embraced Islam in the seventh year of the Hijrah and dedicated himself entirely to accompanying the Prophet and memorizing his teachings. The Prophet testified to his keen memory, and he became the foremost transmitter of the Prophetic Sunnah. His narrations form the backbone of the major hadith collections.

name_en: It was narrated that Abu Hurairah said
bio_short_en: Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the Ahl al-Suffah, the poor companions who lived in the mosque, and the most prolific transmitter of hadith. He memorized an enormous number of the Prophet's sayings and supplicated to Allah to strengthen his memory, which the Prophet confirmed with a gesture. Thousands of narrations from him are preserved in the major collections, making him an indispensable source for knowledge of the Sunnah.

Ibn Buraidah refers to the sons of Buraidah ibn al-Husayb al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him — most commonly Abdullah ibn Buraidah or Sulaiman ibn Buraidah, both of whom were Tabi'in narrators. They transmitted hadiths from their father Buraidah, a companion of the Prophet, as well as from other companions. Their narrations are found in the major hadith collections across a range of topics including prayer, leadership, and personal conduct.

Al-Mughirah bin Shu'bah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet renowned for his intelligence and political acumen, such that he was described as the most cunning of the Arabs. He embraced Islam before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and went on to serve as a governor and military commander. He narrated numerous hadiths from the Prophet and was one of the last companions to die, passing away around 50 AH.

Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i (Amr ibn Abdullah al-Hamdani) was a highly prolific Tabi'i narrator from Kufa who transmitted hadiths from a large number of companions and senior Tabi'in. He is one of the most frequently cited narrators in the hadith collections and was known for his vast knowledge, though later in life his memory weakened somewhat. Scholars of hadith praised him highly and his narrations appear in all the major collections.

Shu'bah ibn al-Hajjaj was one of the most celebrated hadith critics and narrators of the second generation of Muslims (Tabi'in and their successors). He was among the first to systematically scrutinize the reliability of hadith narrators and is considered a founding figure of the science of hadith criticism. His narrations and evaluations of other narrators are foundational references in the major hadith collections.

name_en: Salim and Hamzah, the sons of 'Abdullah bin 'Umar
bio_short_en: Salim and Hamzah were two sons of the great companion Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him. Salim ibn Abdullah was an eminent Tabi'i scholar and jurist, considered one of the seven great jurists of Medina, who transmitted extensively from his father. Hamzah ibn Abdullah also narrated hadiths from his father. Together they represent an important link in transmitting the Sunnah from the household of Umar ibn al-Khattab to subsequent generations.

Musa bin Talhah was the son of the companion Talhah ibn Ubaydullah, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the ten companions promised Paradise. He was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from his father and from other companions. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he was regarded as a reliable transmitter. He lived in Kufa and was known for his scholarship and piety.

name_en: Abu Hurairah [may Allah be pleased with him]
bio_short_en: Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion who spent three years in close companionship with the Prophet, dedicating himself to memorizing and preserving the Prophet's teachings. He is the single most prolific narrator of hadith, with over five thousand narrations attributed to him in the major collections. The Prophet himself acknowledged Abu Hurairah's extraordinary memory when he spread his cloak and then told him to gather it to his chest, after which Abu Hurairah said he never forgot anything.

A'ishah bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her, was the beloved wife of the Prophet and daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first caliph. She is one of the most prolific narrators of hadith and one of the greatest scholars the Muslim community has ever known. She narrated approximately two thousand hadiths and was a primary authority on matters of personal conduct, worship, and the Prophet's private life. Companions and Tabi'in came to her for religious rulings throughout her life.

Abu Qatadah al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina, renowned as one of the Prophet's finest horsemen and a brave fighter in the early Islamic battles. He narrated numerous hadiths from the Prophet on topics including prayer, fasting, and general conduct. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections, and he was held in high esteem by the companions for his courage and devotion.

Abu Khaldah Khalid ibn Dinar al-Ataki was a Tabi'i narrator from Basra who transmitted hadiths from companions and senior Tabi'in. He is mentioned in the chains of transmission of certain hadiths found in the Sunan collections. Scholars of hadith regarded him as an acceptable narrator, and his narrations provide testimony to the religious practices and teachings of the early Muslim community.

Al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad, may Allah be pleased with him, was among the earliest companions to embrace Islam and one of the first horsemen in the Muslim army. His actual lineage was to Amr ibn Tha'labah, but he was adopted by al-Aswad ibn Abd Yaghuth, hence the name Ibn al-Aswad. He participated in the Battle of Badr and other major campaigns and narrated hadiths from the Prophet. He was known for his bravery and steadfastness in the face of persecution before the Hijrah.

AbuAyyub al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was the companion who hosted the Prophet when he first arrived in Medina, an honor that brought him great distinction. His full name was Khalid ibn Zayd ibn Kulayb, and he was from the Banu Najjar clan of the Ansar. He participated in virtually all of the Prophet's campaigns and continued to participate in Islamic military expeditions well into old age, eventually dying near Constantinople while on campaign, where his grave remains a site of reverence.

Uthman ibn Abul-As al-Thaqafi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet appointed by him as the governor of Taif. He is notable for asking the Prophet to teach him to lead his people in prayer and for the Prophet's advice to him about keeping prayers short and accessible to the congregation. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and was a respected figure among the companions for his knowledge and administrative service.

Ka'b ibn Ujrah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and the narrator of the famous hadith about the fidyah (ransom) for shaving the head during ihram due to illness. He also narrated the complete form of the Abrahamic prayer upon the Prophet, which forms part of the tashahhud recited in every prayer. He lived in Medina and later in Kufa, and his narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Abdullah ibn as-Sa'ib, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and a noted reciter of the Quran. He was appointed as a teacher of Quranic recitation in Mecca and was among those who narrated hadiths from the Prophet. His narrations relating to prayer and Quranic recitation are preserved in the Sunan collections, and he was regarded as an authority on the proper recitation of the Quran.

AbuMalik al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ash'ari tribe of Yemen. He is the narrator of the famous hadith beginning 'Purity is half of faith,' which enumerates the virtues of various acts of worship. His narrations are preserved in Sahih Muslim and other major collections, and he was esteemed among the companions for his piety and knowledge of the Prophet's teachings.

Rifa'ah ibn Rafi al-Ansari al-Zuraqi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina and participated in the Battle of Badr. He narrated several hadiths from the Prophet, including narrations about prayer and its proper performance. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections, and he was respected among the companions for his early acceptance of Islam and his participation in the foundational battles.

Uthman ibn Affan, may Allah be pleased with him, was the third Rightly Guided Caliph and one of the most beloved companions of the Prophet. He was married to two of the Prophet's daughters in succession, earning him the title Dhul-Nurayn (possessor of two lights). He narrated many hadiths from the Prophet and is particularly associated with narrations about the virtues of Wudu and prayer. He compiled the standardized written Quran during his caliphate, an act of immense service to Islam.

Abdullah ibn AbuAwfa al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Aslam tribe who participated in the pledge of Ridwan under the tree at Hudaybiyyah. He lived to a very advanced age and was among the last companions to die in Kufa, making him a living link to the Prophet for the people of Iraq. He narrated numerous hadiths and was a major source of Prophetic knowledge for the Tabi'in of Kufa.

Sahl ibn al-Hanzaliyyah al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who participated in the pledge of Ridwan at Hudaybiyyah. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and was known for his piety and dedication to worship. His narrations are found in the Sunan collections and touch upon topics of vigilance, guarding posts, and the ethics of Islamic conduct.

Abdullah ibn az-Zubayr, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the companion Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and the grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq through his mother Asma'. He was the first child born to the Muhajirun in Medina after the Hijrah, which was seen as a blessed sign. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from his father and aunt A'ishah. He later became caliph and ruled from Mecca for several years, known for his asceticism and bravery.

Ata ibn Yasar was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar based in Medina who narrated from numerous companions including Abu Hurairah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and A'ishah, may Allah be pleased with them. He was among the most reliable and knowledgeable of the Tabi'in and was particularly noted for his narrations about worship and ethics. His hadith appear in all the major collections, and he was praised by scholars of hadith for his precision and piety.

Abdullah ibn Ja'far, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's cousin who died as a martyr at the Battle of Mu'tah. He grew up in the Prophet's household after his father's martyrdom and was personally known to the Prophet, who prayed for blessings upon him. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from other companions, and was known for his generosity, a trait inherited from his father.

Abd al-Rahman ibn Samurah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who participated in the Islamic conquests and served as a military commander in Sijistan and Khorasan. The Prophet gave him notable advice not to seek positions of leadership and not to take oaths lightly. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and reflect important guidance from the Prophet on leadership and personal conduct.

Mu'adh ibn Anas al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Juhainah tribe who settled in Egypt after the Islamic conquests. He narrated several hadiths from the Prophet relating to prayer, supplication, and good manners. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections, particularly the Sunan of Abu Dawud, and reflect the practical religious guidance the Prophet offered to his companions.

Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, may Allah be pleased with him, was among the first to embrace Islam and one of the greatest scholars among the companions. He served as the Prophet's personal attendant and was so close to him that the companions considered him part of the Prophet's household. He narrated a large number of hadiths and was the leading religious authority in Kufa during the early caliphate period. He was particularly renowned for his recitation and knowledge of the Quran.

AbuAyyub al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Khalid ibn Zayd, was the companion who had the honor of hosting the Prophet upon his arrival in Medina. The Prophet stayed in his home for several months before the construction of his mosque and residence was completed. He participated in numerous battles and continued to join military expeditions until old age, dying near Constantinople around 52 AH. His narrations from the Prophet are preserved in the major hadith collections.

Jabir ibn Atik al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina. He is known as the narrator of the hadith about the deaths that are considered martyrdom beyond dying in battle, including dying of plague, drowning, and other causes. He participated in the early battles alongside the Prophet, and his narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections.

Awf ibn Malik al-Ashja'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who participated in the Islamic conquests and settled in Syria. He narrated numerous hadiths from the Prophet on topics including prayer, leadership, supplication, and eschatology. He is known for narrating the famous hadith describing the signs of the Hour and the qualities of good and bad leaders. His narrations are found across the major hadith collections.

Ya'la ibn Umayyah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet also known as Ya'la ibn Munabbih al-Tamimi. He narrated several hadiths from the Prophet relating to the rites of Hajj and the rules of ihram. He is known for narrating the account of a man who came to the Prophet asking about performing Hajj while wearing a perfumed garment, and the Prophet's guidance in that situation. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections.

Ikrimah was a prominent Successor scholar and the freed slave (mawla) of Abdullah ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him). He was considered the foremost student of Ibn Abbas in Quranic exegesis and hadith and narrated an enormous number of traditions from him. He traveled widely to transmit knowledge across the Islamic world and his narrations appear in all major hadith collections. While some early scholars debated his reliability on account of reported Kharijite sympathies, the majority of hadith scholars accepted his narrations. He passed away around 104-107 AH.

Adi ibn Hatim al-Ta'i, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the celebrated pre-Islamic Arab leader Hatim al-Ta'i, famous for his generosity. Adi was initially a Christian before embracing Islam and visiting the Prophet, and he went on to become a devoted companion and a prolific narrator of hadith. He was among those who remained steadfast during the apostasy wars and lived to a very advanced age, narrating from the Prophet about matters of faith, hunting, and lawful food.

Umm Kurz al-Ka'biyyah, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet from Mecca who narrated hadiths relating to the aqiqah (the sacrifice made for a newborn). She is particularly known for transmitting the Prophet's guidance on the number of animals to be slaughtered for a newborn boy versus a girl. Her narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections and represent an important source for rulings related to the aqiqah.

Jubayr ibn Mut'im, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Quraysh tribe and a recognized authority on genealogy and tribal lineages. He embraced Islam after the conquest of Mecca. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on theology, prayer, and the attributes of Allah, including the famous narration of the Prophet reciting Surah al-Tur in prayer. His narrations are preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.

Abyad ibn Hammal al-Ma'ribi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who came from the Yemen region. He is particularly known for narrating hadiths about the iqta (land grants) made by the Prophet and for a notable exchange with the Prophet regarding the salt mines of Ma'rib. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections, particularly those of Abu Dawud and al-Tirmidhi.

AbuBarzah al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Nadlah ibn Ubayd, was a companion of the Prophet from the Aslam tribe. He participated in several of the Prophet's military campaigns and was among those who accompanied the Prophet during the Hudaybiyyah expedition. He narrated numerous hadiths on prayer times, personal conduct, and piety, and lived a long life transmitting the Prophet's teachings to later generations.

Thabit bin Adh-Dahhak al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who gave the pledge of allegiance at Hudaybiyyah under the tree. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and is known for transmitting important reports on vows, oaths, and matters of faith. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections including Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Zayd ibn Thabit, may Allah be pleased with him, was the primary scribe of the Prophet's revelations and one of the most learned companions in knowledge of the Quran and its rulings on inheritance. He was entrusted by Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and later Uthman ibn Affan with the monumental task of compiling the written Quran. He also narrated many hadiths from the Prophet and was considered a supreme authority in Medina on matters of law and Quranic sciences.

Hakim ibn Hizam, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet and the nephew of Khadijah, the Prophet's first wife. He embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca and narrated hadiths from the Prophet on trade ethics, transactions, and generosity. He was known for his noble character and generosity even before Islam. He lived to approximately one hundred and twenty years and was a respected narrator whose reports appear in the major collections.

Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who was entrusted with the Prophet's secrets regarding the hypocrites (munafiqin), earning him the title 'keeper of the Prophet's secret.' He was a major narrator of hadiths concerning the trials and tribulations (fitan) of the end times and the signs of the Hour. His narrations on eschatology are among the most important in the hadith collections, and he served as a governor under Umar ibn al-Khattab.

Abu Qilabah al-Jarmi (Abdullah ibn Zayd) was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar from Basra who narrated hadiths from numerous companions. He was known for his piety, his avoidance of sitting with rulers, and his extensive travels in search of hadith. He is cited in all the major collections and was regarded by hadith scholars as a reliable and precise narrator. His narrations cover a broad range of Islamic jurisprudence and ethics.

Al-Miswar ibn Makhramah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who was brought to Medina as a young child and grew up there. He was the nephew of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, one of the ten companions promised Paradise. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from senior companions and was an important narrator in Medina, known for his accurate transmission and his opposition to certain political decisions after the Prophet's death.

Salamah ibn al-Muhabbaq al-Hudhali, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who settled in Basra after the Islamic conquests. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on matters of personal conduct and legal rulings. His narrations are found in the Sunan collections and he is known to hadith scholars as a companion narrator whose reports reflect the practical teachings of the Prophet.

Abu Talhah al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, whose name was Zayd ibn Sahl, was a companion of the Prophet and one of the greatest archers among the Ansar. He was known for his bravery, generosity, and love for the Prophet, famously offering his own body as a shield for the Prophet at the Battle of Uhud. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and is known for the hadith about his garden, Bayraha, which he donated in charity. His narrations are preserved in the major collections.

AbuMusa al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Abdullah ibn Qays, was a companion of the Prophet from Yemen and one of the most distinguished scholars and governors of the early Islamic period. He was appointed governor of Kufa and Basra by Umar ibn al-Khattab and was renowned for his melodious Quran recitation, which the Prophet praised by saying his voice was like a flute of the family of David. He narrated numerous hadiths and was a key religious authority in Iraq.

Abdullah bin Ubaidullah bin Abi Mulaika was a Tabi'i narrator from Mecca who transmitted hadiths from numerous companions and had access to the major companions of Mecca and Medina. He served as a judge in Mecca and was considered a reliable authority in hadith. His narrations appear in the major collections and span various topics of Islamic jurisprudence and practice.

Abdur-Rahman ibn Abi Laila was a highly regarded Tabi'i scholar and jurist from Kufa who narrated from many prominent companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, Bilal, and Ka'b ibn Ujra, may Allah be pleased with them. He served as a judge in Kufa and was considered one of the most learned men of his time in religious law. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections.

Muhammad bin Abi Al-Mujalid was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths from senior Tabi'in and occasionally from companions. He is found in the chains of transmission of certain hadiths in the Sunan collections. Scholars of hadith accepted his narrations, and his reports contribute to the record of the Prophet's guidance as transmitted through the early Muslim community.

Rabi bin Hirash al-Absi was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar from Kufa renowned for his scrupulousness and piety. He narrated hadiths from Ali ibn Abi Talib, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, and other companions. He was known for never laughing after witnessing the washing of a deceased person, reflecting his deep awareness of death. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and he was highly regarded by scholars of hadith for his reliability.

Abu Waqid al-Laithi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet from the Layth tribe. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on matters including the slaughter of animals, supplication, and Islamic conduct. He is known for the narration about the prohibition of cutting meat from a living animal. His narrations are preserved in the Sunan collections and the Muwatta of Imam Malik.

This entry refers to Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, who is the father of Abu Burda ibn Abi Musa. Abu Musa was a companion of the Prophet from Yemen, renowned for his Quranic recitation, his scholarship, and his service as governor of Kufa and Basra under Umar ibn al-Khattab. He is one of the major narrators among the companions, and hadiths transmitted through his son Abu Burda from him form an important strand of the hadith literature.

Humran ibn Aban was the freed slave of Uthman ibn Affan, may Allah be pleased with him, and a major Tabi'i narrator of hadiths from Uthman. He is particularly known for transmitting Uthman's detailed narrations about the proper performance of wudu (ablution) and the virtues of prayer. His narrations are found in Sahih Muslim and the Sunan collections, and he is regarded as a reliable and important link in the chain transmitting Uthman's knowledge to later generations.

Umm Atiyya al-Ansariyya (RA) was a Companion and skilled woman of the Ansar who participated in the burial washing of women who died during the Prophet's time, including Zaynab, the daughter of the Prophet ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ trusted her with these important funeral duties. She narrated several hadiths about the rules of washing the deceased, funeral prayer, and related matters, making her one of the most important sources for Islamic funeral jurisprudence. Her reports are found in both Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim and are relied upon in all four madhabs.

Amr bin Yahya al-Mazini was a Medinan scholar of the second generation (tabi'in) and a grandson of the companion Abu Sa'id al-Khudri. He is well known for narrating the hadith on wudu (ritual purification) from his father Yahya bin Umara, which became foundational in Islamic jurisprudence. Hadith scholars regarded him as trustworthy and precise. He transmitted from several companions and their successors, and his reports appear in the major hadith collections.

Umm Atiyyah al-Ansariyyah, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet from the Ansar of Medina. She participated in several of the Prophet's military campaigns in the role of nursing the wounded and caring for the sick. She is a primary narrator of hadiths relating to the ritual washing of the deceased, menstruation, and other matters of particular relevance to women. Her narrations are preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.

Ammar refers most commonly to Ammar bin Yasir, may Allah be pleased with him, though the name appears without a patronymic in some chains. Where context is unclear, this entry serves as a placeholder for a companion or successor by this name who transmitted prophetic traditions. His reports appear in the major hadith collections and pertain to various aspects of worship and conduct.

Abdullah bin al-Harith bin Nawfal al-Hashimi was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and a member of the Banu Hashim clan. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet as well as from senior companions including Umar and Uthman, may Allah be pleased with them. He served as a governor in Basra and was regarded as a reliable narrator. His reports are found in the major hadith collections.

Zaid bin Wahb al-Juhani was a tabi'i (successor generation) scholar who traveled to Medina to meet the Prophet but arrived after the Prophet's passing, may Allah have mercy on him. He was a close associate of Ali bin Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him, and narrated from many companions including Hudhaifa, Abu Dharr, and Ibn Masud. He is considered highly reliable by hadith scholars and his narrations are found throughout the major collections.

Abu Bakr As-Siddiq, may Allah be pleased with him, was the closest companion and father-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and the first Caliph of Islam. He was the first adult male to embrace Islam and accompanied the Prophet during the Hijra to Medina. Though his tenure as Caliph was brief (632–634 CE), he consolidated the Muslim community and initiated the compilation of the Quran. He narrated numerous hadiths that are recorded across all major collections.

Uqba, where referenced without further qualification in hadith chains, typically refers to Uqba bin Amir al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, or occasionally to another companion of the same name. This entry serves as a general placeholder for narrators identified only as Uqba in a given chain. The most prominent narrator by this name is the companion Uqba bin Amir al-Juhani, whose narrations appear in all major collections.

Yahya bin Sa'id al-Ansari was a distinguished tabi'i scholar and judge of Medina. He was one of the most relied-upon transmitters of his generation, narrating from Anas bin Malik, Sa'id bin al-Musayyib, and many other senior successors. Imam Malik and other major imams transmitted from him extensively. Hadith scholars unanimously praised his precision and integrity, and his narrations form a cornerstone of the Muwatta and the Sahihayn.

As-Sa'ib bin Yazid (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who was brought to the Prophet as a child during the Farewell Pilgrimage. He is among the Companions who lived long enough to narrate directly from the Prophet and remain a link to the prophetic era well into the Umayyad period. He narrated hadiths related to weights, measures, and commerce in Medina, and is known for his accounts of the market of Medina. He passed away around 91 AH.

Fatima bint al-Mundhir was a tabi'iyyah (female successor) and the wife of the great jurist Hisham bin Urwa. She was the granddaughter of the companion al-Zubayr bin al-Awwam, may Allah be pleased with him, and narrated hadiths from her grandmother Asma bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her. She is cited as a trustworthy narrator and her transmissions appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and other major collections.

Amr bin Taghlib, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. He narrated a small number of hadiths, including a well-known report about the Prophet's distribution of wealth and the importance of satisfying hearts in Islam. His narrations are found in Sahih al-Bukhari. He is regarded as a reliable companion narrator.

A'isha bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her, was the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and one of the greatest scholars among his companions. She is the third-highest transmitter of hadith overall, narrating over two thousand reports preserved in all major collections. She was a leading authority on matters of worship, family law, and the Prophet's private conduct. After the Prophet's passing she served as a teacher to generations of Muslims for nearly fifty years.

Amir bin Rabi'a al-Anzi, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the early companions who embraced Islam before the migration to Abyssinia. He participated in both Hijras and was among the most dedicated early Muslims. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet, peace be upon him, including well-known narrations on funeral prayers and greeting the deceased. His reports appear in the six major hadith collections.

Kharija bin Zaid bin Thabit was a prominent Medinan tabi'i and one of the seven great jurists of Medina known collectively as the Fuqaha' al-Sab'ah. He was the son of the famous companion and Quran scribe Zaid bin Thabit, may Allah be pleased with him, and inherited his father's mastery of Islamic law and Quran sciences. He is considered highly reliable by hadith scholars and his narrations appear in the major collections.

Amir, where cited without further specification in hadith chains, most commonly refers to Amir bin Shurahbil al-Sha'bi, a leading tabi'i scholar and jurist of Kufa, or occasionally to a companion named Amir. Al-Sha'bi was renowned for his encyclopedic knowledge, legal acumen, and sharp memory. He narrated from hundreds of companions and is considered one of the most authoritative transmitters of the successor generation. His narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections.

Musa bin Uqba al-Asadi was a Medinan tabi'i scholar best known for his pioneering work in Islamic historiography, particularly his early Sira (biography of the Prophet). He was a freed slave of the household of al-Zubayr and narrated from major successors including Nafi' and Urwa bin al-Zubayr. Imam Malik praised his Sira highly. His hadith narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Muslim.

Abu Humaid Al-Sa'idi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, from the Ansar of Medina. He is particularly known for narrating detailed descriptions of the Prophet's prayer, which are cited as authoritative in books of jurisprudence. His narration describing the prayer postures step by step is preserved in Abu Dawud and other collections. He is regarded as a reliable and precise companion narrator.

Ziyad bin Jubair was a tabi'i narrator from Basra who transmitted from companions including Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them. He is known for narrating reports related to prayer, Hajj, and trade. Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim as well as the Sunan collections.

Abdur-Rahman bin al-Qasim bin Muhammad bin Abi Bakr was a leading Medinan tabi'i scholar and the primary transmitter of the jurisprudence of his father al-Qasim bin Muhammad, one of the seven great Medinan jurists. He transmitted extensively from his father, who took knowledge from his grandmother A'isha, may Allah be pleased with her. Imam Malik relied heavily on his narrations in the Muwatta. He is considered one of the most reliable Medinan authorities.

name_en: Zaid bin Aslam from his father
bio_short_en: Zaid bin Aslam transmitted a number of hadith reports from his father Aslam, a freed slave and close companion of Umar bin al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him. Aslam witnessed many events during Umar's caliphate and preserved important narrations. Zaid bin Aslam was himself a prominent tabi'i scholar, and his reports from his father provide a valuable link to the practices of the second caliph. Their combined chain appears in the Muwatta and other collections.

Abdullah bin Abu Qatada was the son of the companion Abu Qatada al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the Prophet's horsemen and a major narrator of hadith. Abdullah narrated from his father, transmitting important reports about prayer, fasting, and prophetic conduct. His narrations are found in Sahih Muslim and other major collections. He is considered a reliable member of the successor generation.

Abu Humaid, referenced without a nisba in some chains, most commonly refers to Abu Humaid al-Sa'idi, a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, known for his detailed narration of the Prophet's prayer. Where the reference is ambiguous, this entry serves as a general placeholder. The narrations attributed to Abu Humaid in the canonical collections are authoritative and frequently cited in jurisprudential discussions.

Thabit bin Aslam al-Bunani was a distinguished tabi'i scholar from Basra, famous for his deep piety, lengthy prayers, and close association with the companion Anas bin Malik, may Allah be pleased with him. He is considered one of the most reliable transmitters from Anas and narrated an enormous number of hadiths from him. Imam Ahmad and other hadith masters praised him without reservation. His narrations are abundant in all major hadith collections.

Yahya, when cited without further specification, typically refers to one of several prominent transmitters: most often Yahya bin Sa'id al-Ansari, the authoritative Medinan tabi'i, or Yahya bin Sa'id al-Qattan, the celebrated Basran hadith critic. Both are highly reliable. This entry serves as a general reference; the specific identity depends on the chain context. Both scholars' narrations appear extensively throughout the canonical collections.

Abdullah bin Abi Mulaika al-Taymi was a tabi'i scholar of Mecca who served as a judge and muezzin during the caliphate of Ibn al-Zubayr. He narrated from many companions, including A'isha, Ibn Abbas, and Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them. Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy and knowledgeable. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.

Abdullah bin Abi Awfa, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the last to pass away in Kufa. He participated in the pledge of Ridwan and many military campaigns. He went blind in his old age and continued to teach until late in his life, becoming an important source for companions' narrations in Kufa. His hadiths appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Uqba bin Amir al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad known for his knowledge of the Quran, skill in archery, and dedication to the path of Islam. He served as governor of Egypt and transmitted many hadiths on prayer, purification, charity, and the importance of archery in Islamic practice. He is also known for narrating the Prophet's words on the obligations of Muslims toward one another. He passed away in Egypt around 58 AH.

Anas ibn Malik (RA) served the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the age of ten for ten years, giving him intimate and sustained knowledge of the Prophet's daily life and practice. He was one of the most prolific narrators of hadith among the companions and lived to an advanced age, transmitting knowledge to many Tabi'i scholars who came to learn from him. His narrations are a primary source in all major hadith collections, particularly for details of the Prophet's personal conduct, worship, and manners.

Abdullah bin Ka'b bin Malik was a tabi'i and the son of the famous companion Ka'b bin Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the three companions who stayed behind from the Tabuk expedition and whose repentance is mentioned in the Quran. Abdullah narrated from his father and transmitted important reports about that well-known event as well as other aspects of prophetic practice. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections.

Sa'id bin Zaid, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the ten companions promised Paradise by the Prophet, peace be upon him, and an early convert to Islam. He was the husband of the Prophet's paternal aunt Fatima bint al-Khattab and participated in nearly all major military campaigns. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and his narrations are preserved in the major collections. He is counted among the greatest companions.

Samura bin Jundab, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who embraced Islam at a young age. He is known for his large collection of narrations and served as a governor in Basra during the caliphate of Muawiyah, may Allah be pleased with him. He transmitted many hadiths covering a wide range of topics and his reports appear in the major hadith collections, including Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Abu Umama al-Bahili, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the most prolific narrators from the Syrian region. He settled in Homs and later in Damascus, where he became an important transmitter of prophetic traditions to the people of Greater Syria. He lived to an old age and narrated widely, with his reports appearing in the six major hadith collections.

Ya'li, likely referring to Ya'la bin Umaiya or Ya'la bin Murra, may Allah be pleased with them, were companions of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who narrated a number of hadiths. Ya'la bin Umaiya participated in several military campaigns and served as an administrator. His narrations appear in the major Sunan collections. This entry serves as a general reference where the full name is unspecified in the chain.

Sahl bin Abi Hathma, may Allah be pleased with him, was a young companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, from the Ansar of Medina. He is particularly known for narrating the detailed hadith regarding the blood money case (qasama) between the Ansar and the Jews of Khaybar, which is an important legal precedent. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim. He is regarded as a reliable companion narrator.

Malik bin Sasaa, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the narrators of the detailed account of the Night Journey (Isra wal-Mi'raj). His narration of this event is preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari and is one of the primary sources for the description of that miraculous journey. He is regarded as a reliable companion narrator, and his report on this topic is of great theological significance.

Abdullah bin Zama'a al-Qurashi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, whose narrations are found in the major hadith collections. He is known for transmitting reports related to the Prophet's conduct and rulings. He was among those who were present at the death of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. His narrations are preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari and other collections.

Abdullah bin Amr bin al-As, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most prolific companions in terms of hadith narration, having been authorized by the Prophet to write down hadiths directly. He was the son of the famous general Amr bin al-As and was known for his great asceticism, extensive worship, and scholarly dedication. His written collection, known as al-Sahifa al-Sadiqa, is considered one of the earliest hadith compilations. His narrations are found in all major collections.

Abu Masud al-Ansari (RA), also known as Uqba ibn Amr al-Badri, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in the pledge of Aqabah and was among the early supporters of Islam. He narrated several hadiths on topics including prayer, leadership, and the qualities of a Muslim, and is cited in the major hadith collections. He later settled in Kufa and transmitted prophetic knowledge to the scholars there, where he was respected for his reliability and piety.

Hudhaifa bin al-Yaman, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who held the unique distinction of being the keeper of the Prophet's secrets concerning the hypocrites (munafiqin). He is well known for narrating hadiths about fitna (tribulations) and the signs of the Last Hour, and the caliph Umar would consult him about hypocrites. He later settled in Kufa and became one of its most important scholars. His narrations appear in all major collections.

Ubaidullah bin Adi bin al-Khiyar was a prominent tabi'i scholar of Medina from the Quraysh tribe. He narrated from several companions including Uthman bin Affan, Ali bin Abi Talib, and Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with them. He is particularly known for his narration of the story of the hypocrite whom the Prophet ordered be killed. Hadith scholars considered him reliable and his narrations appear in the major collections.

Qais bin Abi Hazim was a leading tabi'i scholar who had the distinction of being the only person to have narrated from each of the ten companions promised Paradise. He narrated from Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, and many other senior companions, may Allah be pleased with them all. He was a Kufan scholar of great standing and is considered among the most authoritative transmitters of the successor generation. His narrations appear in all major hadith collections.

Usaid bin Hudair, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the chiefs of the Aws tribe of the Ansar in Medina. He participated in the Second Pledge of Aqaba and was among the most respected of the Ansar. He is known for his courage, his love of the Quran, and several narrations about the descent of divine peace and angels when reciting Quran. His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Sufyan, cited without further specification, typically refers to one of two major hadith scholars: Sufyan al-Thawri (d. 161 AH), the Kufan imam and master of hadith criticism, or Sufyan bin Uyayna (d. 198 AH), the Meccan scholar and major teacher of Imam al-Shafi'i. Both are considered among the greatest authorities in hadith. This entry is a general placeholder; the specific Sufyan is determined by chain context. Their narrations appear throughout all major collections.

Ammar bin Yasir, may Allah be pleased with him, was among the earliest converts to Islam and endured severe persecution alongside his parents. His mother Sumayya is considered the first martyr in Islam. The Prophet, peace be upon him, praised Ammar highly and declared that a rebellious group would kill him, which came to pass at the Battle of Siffin. He is regarded as one of the foremost companions and his narrations are found in all major hadith collections.

Yazid bin Abi Ubaid was a freed slave (mawla) of Salama bin al-Akwa, may Allah be pleased with him, and a tabi'i narrator. He transmitted hadiths from Salama and from other companions, covering topics of prayer, fasting, and military campaigns. Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy and reliable. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim among other collections.

Zaid bin Aslam al-Adawi was a prominent Medinan tabi'i scholar and a freed slave of Umar bin al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated from his father Aslam, from Umar's children and household, and from many companions including Jabir and Abu Hurayra. He was also known as a Quran exegete whose tafsir was transmitted by his son Abdur-Rahman. Imam Malik narrated from him extensively. His reports appear in the major hadith collections.

Safwan bin Ya'la bin Umaiya was the son of the companion Ya'la bin Umaiya, may Allah be pleased with him, and narrated hadiths from his father. Ya'la bin Umaiya was a companion who participated in military campaigns and served as an administrator. Safwan transmitted his father's narrations to the next generation, and his reports appear in the major Sunan and Sahih collections. He is considered a reliable tabi'i narrator.

Safwan bin Muhriz al-Mazini was a tabi'i scholar of Basra known for his piety, extensive worship, and weeping out of fear of Allah. He narrated from Imran bin Husayn and other companions, may Allah be pleased with them. Hadith scholars praised his reliability and sincerity. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim, particularly regarding hadiths about accountability on the Day of Judgment.

Yusuf bin Mahak was a Meccan tabi'i scholar who narrated from several companions including A'isha, Abu Hurayra, and Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with them. He is known for reporting how the Quran was originally arranged in the mushaf (codex). Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and the Sunan collections.

Zirr bin Hubaish al-Asadi was a prominent tabi'i scholar of Kufa known for his mastery of the Arabic language and the Quran. He narrated from companions including Ali bin Abi Talib, Uthman, Ibn Masud, and Ubayy bin Ka'b, may Allah be pleased with them. He was particularly known for his study of Quranic recitation directly from senior companions. He lived to an extremely old age and is considered a highly reliable narrator.

Abdullah bin Ja'far, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Ja'far bin Abi Talib, the cousin of the Prophet who was martyred at the Battle of Mu'ta. He was raised in the household of the Prophet, peace be upon him, after his father's martyrdom, and is reported to have received the Prophet's blessings. He was known for his generosity and nobility. His narrations from the Prophet appear in the major hadith collections.

Saud, referenced without further specification in some hadith chains, likely refers to a tabi'i or companion transmitter by this name. Without additional biographical details from the chain context, a precise identification is uncertain. This entry serves as a placeholder for narrators identified only as Saud in a given isnad. Readers should consult the specific chain for more precise identification.

Al-A'raj, whose full name was Abdur-Rahman bin Hurmuz, was one of the leading tabi'i scholars of Medina and among the most prolific transmitters from Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with him. He was also a master of the Quran and Arabic language. Imam al-Zuhri and Malik relied heavily on his narrations, and he is considered among the most authoritative of Medinan tabi'in. His reports appear extensively in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Ali bin Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him, was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and the fourth Caliph of Islam. He was among the first to embrace Islam, fought in nearly every major battle alongside the Prophet, and was renowned for his knowledge, bravery, and eloquence. He narrated a large number of hadiths from the Prophet and is one of the most important companion narrators. His reports appear in all major hadith collections.

Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most prolific narrators among the companions of the Prophet, peace be upon him, transmitting over a thousand hadiths. He was an Ansari companion who participated in twelve military campaigns and lived in Medina until late in his life, becoming one of the primary sources for prophetic traditions in that city. His narrations cover theology, worship, eschatology, and ethics. They appear in all major hadith collections.

Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, Abdullah bin Abbas was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and is known as the 'Scholar of the Ummah' (Habr al-Umma) for his extraordinary mastery of Quran, tafsir, and hadith. The Prophet supplicated for him to be given understanding of the religion, and he fulfilled that du'a magnificently. He narrated thousands of hadiths and his tafsir is among the most authoritative. His narrations appear in all major collections.

Jabir bin Samurah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and the son of Samurah bin Jundab's brother. He settled in Kufa and became one of its important sources of prophetic traditions. He narrated numerous hadiths on a wide range of topics including prayer, the characteristics of the Prophet, and eschatology. His narrations are found in Sahih Muslim and across the other major hadith collections.

Zaid bin Arqam, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who participated in seventeen military campaigns. He is particularly known for narrating the famous hadith of Ghadir Khumm and various reports about the Prophet's family and companions. He settled in Kufa and became a major source for prophetic traditions in Iraq. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.

Wa'il bin Hujr, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and a nobleman from Yemen who came to accept Islam. The Prophet honored him and prayed for blessings upon him and his descendants. He is particularly known for narrating detailed descriptions of the Prophet's prayer, including hand placement and recitation. His narrations on prayer are frequently cited in fiqh literature and appear in the major Sunan collections.

Hudhaifa bin al-Yaman, may Allah be pleased with him, was the trusted confidant of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who kept the list of hypocrites secret at the Prophet's request. He is renowned for his narrations concerning tribulations (fitan), the signs of the Last Hour, and the characteristics of the Dajjal. The caliph Umar and other companions would consult him on matters of hypocrisy. He settled in Kufa and his narrations appear in all major hadith collections.

Abu Bakra, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Nufay bin al-Harith, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who liberated himself from slavery in Taif and joined the Muslims. He settled in Basra and became one of the most important sources of prophetic traditions in that city, narrating over a hundred hadiths. He is known for his narrations on leadership, justice, and prophetic guidance. His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Abdullah bin Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the second Caliph Umar bin al-Khattab and one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting over two thousand reports. He was renowned for his meticulous following of the Prophet's Sunnah in every detail of life and was considered an authority in both hadith and Islamic law. He lived to an advanced age in Medina and remained a primary source for prophetic traditions for the successor generation. His narrations are central to all major collections.

Abu Bakrah al-Thaqafi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who is also known as Nufay bin al-Harith al-Thaqafi. He settled in Basra after the Muslim conquests and narrated many important hadiths. He is known for the famous narration in which the Prophet said, 'Do not return to disbelief after me by striking each other's necks.' His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.

Ma'qil bin Yasar al-Muzani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, from the Muzayna tribe. He participated in the pledge under the tree (Bay'at al-Ridwan) and in military campaigns. He is particularly known for narrating the famous hadith recommending the recitation of Surah Yasin for the dying. He settled in Basra and his reports appear in the major hadith collections including Abu Dawud and al-Tirmidhi.

Ubada bin As-Samit, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the chiefs of the Khazraj tribe of the Ansar. He participated in the First and Second Pledges of Aqaba and is the narrator of the famous hadith on the conditions of the pledge of allegiance to the Prophet. He later served as a teacher and governor in Syria. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and other collections.

Abu az-Zubair Muhammad bin Muslim al-Makki was a tabi'i scholar of Mecca who was one of the primary transmitters from the companion Jabir bin Abdillah, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated an enormous number of hadiths from Jabir and is the main source for many of Jabir's reports in Sahih Muslim. Hadith scholars discussed his narrations critically; Imam Muslim used him extensively while others were more cautious. His narrations appear prominently in the Sahihayn.

Abu Abdur-Rahman Abdullah bin Habib al-Sulami was a prominent tabi'i scholar of Kufa renowned for his mastery of Quranic recitation. He learned the Quran directly from Uthman, Ali, Ibn Masud, and Zayd bin Thabit, may Allah be pleased with them, and taught it in Kufa for forty years during the caliphate of Ali and beyond. He narrated hadiths from several companions and is considered a highly reliable authority. His narrations appear in the major collections.

Abu Tha'laba al-Khushani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who participated in the Treaty of Hudaybiyya and the pledge under the tree. He settled in Syria after the Muslim conquests and became one of the important narrators of prophetic traditions in the region. He narrated hadiths on a variety of topics including hunting, food, and religious obligations. His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan.

Fadalah bin Ubaid al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who later served as a judge and governor in Damascus under Muawiyah bin Abi Sufyan, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on a variety of topics. He was known for his piety and judicial wisdom. His narrations appear in the major Sunan collections and in some narrations of Sahih Muslim.

Abdul-Malik bin Umair al-Lakhmi al-Qurashi was a long-lived tabi'i scholar of Kufa who narrated from many companions including Jabir bin Samurah, al-Mughira bin Shu'ba, and others. He was a skilled orator and narrator who lived into the era of the atba' al-tabi'in (the generation after the successors). Hadith scholars generally considered him trustworthy though they noted some issues with his memory in old age. His narrations appear in all major collections.

Usamah bin Zaid, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Zaid bin Haritha, the Prophet's freed slave and beloved companion, and was himself deeply loved by the Prophet, peace be upon him, who called him 'the beloved son of the beloved.' He was appointed by the Prophet to lead a military expedition just before the Prophet's passing. He narrated numerous hadiths and is considered one of the noble companions. His reports appear in all six major hadith collections.

Atiyyah al-Awfi was a tabi'i scholar of Kufa who narrated from companions including Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with them. He is known for his tafsir narrations and his transmission of Abu Sa'id's reports. Some hadith scholars noted concerns about the reliability of some of his narrations, while others accepted him. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are frequently cited in Quranic commentary literature.
